subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fibroid pseudocapsule, most/least vascular part, and where degeneration begins?
back: Pseudocapsule covers the fibroid and houses its supplying vessels. Most vascular: periphery; least: centre. Degeneration begins from the centre.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fibroid degenerations: most common, the one beginning at periphery, X-ray sign, least common?
back: M/c: hyaline. Calcareous degeneration begins at periphery (m/c in subserosal) — X-ray popcorn calcification/tombstone appearance. Least common: malignant transformation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Red degeneration of fibroid: when, features, cause, Ix, management?
back: Second half of pregnancy; abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting; aseptic (thrombosis in supplying vessels); Ix WBC↑, ESR↑; Mx conservative (analgesics, antiemetics) — no antibiotics, no TOP, no myomectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Submucous fibroid causing HMB/infertility: surgery by FIGO type?
back: Type 0 & 1: hysteroscopic myomectomy. Type 2: laparoscopic myomectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Intramural/subserosal fibroid with HMB: 1st-line vs 2nd-line drugs?
back: 1st line (don't affect size): tranexamic acid, OCP, progesterone. 2nd line (↓size & bleeding): ↓estrogen (danazol, continuous GnRH analogue, GnRH antagonist, letrozole) and ↓progesterone (mifepristone/RU-486, ulipristal—SPRM).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If medical management fails in subserosal/intramural fibroid: surgery by family status?
back: Family not complete: laparoscopic myomectomy. Family complete: UAE / HIFU / MRgFUS / hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Myomectomy routes, delivery after, and UAE details/contraindications?
back: Hysteroscopic (type 0,1) or laparoscopic (type 2, subserosal, intramural). After myomectomy, delivery by LSCS. UAE: via femoral artery using polyvinyl alcohol; C/I in women wanting future pregnancy and menopausal women.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fibroid on P/A and P/V examination?
back: P/A: abdominopelvic lump—cannot converge fingers below it, mobile firm midline mass, irregularly enlarged up to ~20 wks. P/V: irregularly enlarged, non-tender uterus ~up to 20 wks. TVS is IOC; doppler shows ↑vascularity around periphery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Endometrial polyp: bleeding pattern, gross features, doppler sign, IOC, management?
back: Reproductive age: irregular bleeding (metrorrhagia, never in fibroid); menopausal: m/c cause of postmenopausal bleed. Gross: red fleshy outgrowth, narrow base, single feeder vessel. Doppler: feeder vessel sign. IOC: hysteroscopy. Mx: endometrial → hysteroscopic polypectomy; endocervical → polypectomy hook.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Adenomyosis: who, symptoms, P/V findings?
back: ≥40 yrs, multiparous (m/c). Symptoms: menorrhagia + dysmenorrhea, uterine tenderness. P/V: globular, symmetrically enlarged, tender uterus (Halban sign), 10–12 wk size, no adnexal tenderness.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Adenomyosis: diagnosis, IOC, transition-zone cut-offs, USG signs, management, gold standard?
back: Dx clinical; IOC MRI. Transition zone ≥12 mm = adenomyosis, <8 mm = no adenomyosis. USG: myometrial cyst, 'venetian blind' appearance (alternating dark/light bands). Mx: total abdominal hysterectomy. Gold standard Ix: HPE.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: True vs pseudo broad-ligament fibroid — origin and ureter relation?
back: True: arises from broad ligament, ureter is medial. Pseudo: subserous fibroid growing towards BL, ureter is lateral.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Characteristic USG findings: ovarian abscess / hemorrhagic cyst / hydrosalpinx?
back: Ovarian abscess: heterogeneous internal echoes. Hemorrhagic cyst: fishnet/reticular appearance. Hydrosalpinx: retort-shaped tube.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Genital TB: commonest primary source, route of spread, and commonest/2nd/least common genital sites?
back: Usually secondary. M/c primary: lungs > lymph nodes > bones; spread hematogenous. Genital sites: fallopian tube/ampulla (m/c) > endometrium (2nd) > vagina & vulva (l/c). M/c PID in virgin females.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Genital TB in the fallopian tube and endometrium — effects?
back: FT: m/c ampulla, 2nd isthmus; B/L disease → fibrosis, strictures, adhesions, thick wall + narrow lumen. Endometrium: spreads directly → pyometra, Asherman syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Genital TB presentations in order of frequency?
back: 1st infertility; 2nd pain; 3rd menstrual irregularity (1st menorrhagia, m/c secondary amenorrhea); 4th adnexal mass.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Genital TB examination findings (P/A and P/V)?
back: P/A: doughy abdomen. P/V: m/c normal; pelvic tenderness, retroverted fixed uterus, rarely adnexal mass.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Genital TB: investigations (incl. virgin female) and management?
back: HSG done AFTER starting ATT. Confirm: endometrial biopsy → HPE + AFB. Virgin female: menstrual-blood PCR on day 1. Mx: ATT ×6 months (can improve fertility); if tubes distorted → IVF.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HSG: dye, instrument, timing, contraindications?
back: Dye: Urografin (iodinated, water-soluble). Instrument: Leech-Wilkinson cannula. Day 7–10 of cycle. C/I: pregnancy, PID, genital TB.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HSG: coincidental finding, main indication, and causes of B/L cornual block?
back: Coincidental: Müllerian malformation. IOC for tubal patency. B/L cornual block: physiological (m/c, due to pain) or pathological (genital TB).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HSG findings in genital TB?
back: B/L cornual block, lead-pipe appearance, cotton-wool appearance, tobacco-pouch appearance, beaded appearance of tube, golf-stick appearance, Asherman changes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HSG filling-defect pattern: Asherman vs polyp/fibroid?
back: Asherman: multiple defects, irregular margins, moth-eaten. Polyp/fibroid: single regular defect.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Physiological discharge (leucorrhea) features, and general vaginitis IOC/gold standard/pH rule?
back: Leucorrhea: colorless, odourless, no pruritus (discharge from cervix). Vaginitis IOC: saline microscopy. Gold standard: culture (except bacterial vaginosis → Gram stain). pH always >4.5 EXCEPT Candida <4.5.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Bacterial vaginosis: flora change, discharge, whiff test, IOC, gold standard, DOC, partner Rx?
back: Döderlein bacilli replaced by Gardnerella & Mobiluncus. Discharge greyish-white, pH ≥4.5, no pruritus, fishy odour. Whiff test (+10% KOH): amine odour. IOC saline microscopy (clue cells). Gold standard Gram stain → Nugent ≥7. DOC metronidazole. Partner Rx not required.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Amsel's criteria for bacterial vaginosis?
back: Any 3 of: greyish-white foul-smelling discharge; pH ≥4.5; clue cells ≥20%; whiff test +ve. (Gardnerella = gram-variable coccobacilli; clue cell = bacteria adhered to epithelial cells.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Trichomonas vaginitis: discharge, P/S sign, IOC, gold standard, DOC, partner Rx?
back: Trichomonas vaginalis. Discharge frothy yellowish-green, pH ≥4.5, pruritus +, dysuria. P/S strawberry cervix; whiff +/-. IOC saline microscopy (motility +). Gold standard culture (Stuart media). DOC metronidazole. Partner Rx required (STD).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Candidiasis: discharge, pH, main complaint, IOC, gold standard, DOC, partner Rx?
back: Discharge curdy-white/cottage-cheese, pH <4.5, main complaint pruritus, a/w splash dysuria, P/S white flakes. IOC saline microscopy (pseudohyphae). Gold standard culture. DOC fluconazole. Partner Rx only if symptomatic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Candidiasis: DOC in pregnancy and groups where it's the commonest vaginitis?
back: DOC in pregnancy: topical imidazoles (clotrimazole) — oral azoles C/I. M/c vaginitis in pregnancy (acidic pH), diabetics, antibiotic/steroid/OCP use, immunocompromised.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Epithelial cell : polymorph (PMN) ratio in trichomonas/candidiasis vs bacterial vaginosis?
back: Trichomonas & candidiasis: <1. Bacterial vaginosis: >1.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PID: which structures, commonest cause overall, and in acute/virgin/IUCD/OCP users?
back: Upper genital tract: uterus + ovary + tubes + peritoneum. M/c overall: multi-organism; 2nd chlamydia. Acute PID: gonorrhea. Virgin: genital TB. IUCD users: actinomyces. OCP users: chlamydia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: DOC for chlamydia vs gonorrhea?
back: Chlamydia: azithromycin 2 g single OR doxycycline 100 mg BD ×7 d (treat symptomatic partner). Gonorrhea: inj. ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single OR cefixime 800 mg single.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: CDC minimum criteria for PID diagnosis?
back: Lower abdominal pain WITH any of: uterine tenderness, adnexal tenderness, cervical motion tenderness.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: CDC additional and specific criteria for PID?
back: Additional: fever, mucopurulent discharge, abundant WBC on microscopy, raised ESR, raised CRP, chlamydia/gonorrhea test. Specific: endometrial biopsy (endometritis), TVS/MRI, laparoscopy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PID USG signs (acute vs chronic)?
back: Adhesions in tube/peritoneal cavity, hydrosalpinx (retort-shaped tube), beads-on-string, waist sign (chronic PID), cogwheel sign (acute PID).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Role of laparoscopy in PID, and the conception score?
back: Gold standard investigation; directly visualises tubes/adnexa; allows specimen collection. Boer-Meisel score for conception — does NOT include type of organism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Gainesville staging of PID?
back: Stage 1: no peritonitis. Stage 2: peritonitis present. Stage 3: tubo-ovarian mass/abscess. Stage 4: ruptured tubo-ovarian mass.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome?
back: Perihepatitis → adhesions between liver and anterior abdomen (violin-string appearance); m/c in chlamydia > gonorrhea.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PID long-term consequences and uncommon complications?
back: Consequences: infertility (m/c), ectopic pregnancy, hydrosalpinx, chronic pelvic pain, recurrent PID. Uncommon: pyometra, malignancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IUCD + PID management?
back: Routine: antibiotics WITHOUT IUCD removal. Persistent PID / patient demand: IUCD removal + antibiotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Syndromic Mx — vaginal discharge: kit number/colour, drugs, partner Rx?
back: Kit 2 (green): secnidazole 2 g stat (trichomonas/BV) + fluconazole 150 mg stat (candidiasis). Partner Rx: not given (given only if trichomonas positive).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Syndromic Mx — cervical discharge: kit number/colour, drugs, partner Rx?
back: Kit 1 (grey): azithromycin 1 g stat + cefixime 800 mg stat. Partner Rx: kit 1.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Syndromic Mx — PID: kit number/colour, drugs, partner Rx?
back: Kit 6 (yellow): doxycycline 100 mg BD ×14 d + metronidazole 400 mg BD ×14 d + cefixime 800 mg stat. Partner Rx: kit 1.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Age groups: CIN vs carcinoma in-situ vs cancer cervix?
back: CIN: 21–30 yrs. Carcinoma in-situ: 30–35 yrs. Cancer cervix: 35–39 and 60–65 yrs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Risk factors for cervical cancer/CIN, and factors that are NOT risk factors?
back: Risk: HPV (most important), early coitarche <18, early pregnancy <20, multiple partners, multiparity, HIV, low socioeconomic status, smoking, OCP (adenocarcinoma cervix). NOT risk factors: early menarche/late menopause, familial inheritance, HRT.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is CIN diagnosed, and LSIL/HSIL ↔ CIN grades with malignancy risk?
back: CIN diagnosed on BIOPSY (not Pap smear). LSIL → CIN 1 (1% malignancy). HSIL → CIN 2 (5%) and CIN 3 (15–30%).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of CIN 1 vs CIN 2/3 vs CIN extending to vagina?
back: CIN 1: usually regresses → follow up in 2 yrs; if not → cryoablation. CIN 2/3 (any age/parity): LEEP/LLETZ. Extends to vagina: laser; hysterectomy never done.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cryoablation: agent, anaesthesia, m/c complication, depth, and criteria?
back: Cold gas CO2/N2O at very low temp; no anaesthesia, no bleeding; m/c complication persistent watery discharge; destroys epithelium 5 mm deep. Criteria: entire dysplastic area visible on exocervix (not endocervix) and visible lesion <75% of ectocervix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: LEEP/LLETZ: setting, depth, and why preferred over cryoablation?
back: OPD, short procedure; destroys epithelium up to 10 mm deep; preferred because tissue is obtained for diagnosis; very low bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HPV high-risk subtypes: which causes 70% of cervical cancer, m/c for squamous vs adenocarcinoma, others?
back: HPV 16 → 70% of cancer cervix (m/c for squamous cell). HPV 18 → m/c for adenocarcinoma of cervix. Other high-risk: 31, 33, 45, 52, 58.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HPV-related cancers in females vs males, and low-risk strains?
back: Females: cervix, vagina, vulva. Males: penis, oral, anal. Low-risk HPV 6 & 11 → genital warts, condyloma acuminata, laryngeal papilloma in newborn.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HPV proteins: viral replication, malignant transformation, vaccine?
back: Replication: E1, E2. Malignant transformation: E6 (inactivates p53), E7 (inactivates RB). Vaccine made from: L1 capsid. HPV is epitheliotropic, infects parabasal cells.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HPV vaccine: age group, ideal age, who can/can't receive, and CIN-1 note?
back: Age 9–26 (high-risk females 27–45); ideal 11–12 yrs. Can give to boys, sexually active/HPV+/HIV+ females. Cannot give to pregnant females; cannot treat CIN 1. After vaccination, screening must continue.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Bivalent vaccine (Cervarix): coverage, protection, dosing?
back: HPV 16, 18; protects against CIN & cancer cervix. Dosing: <15 yrs 2 doses (0,6 m); ≥15 yrs 3 doses (0,2,6 m).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Nonavalent (Gardasil-9): coverage, age approval, protection?
back: HPV 6,11,16,18,31,33,45,52; approved up to 45 yrs; protects against CIN/cancer cervix, genital warts, vulval & vaginal Ca.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Quadrivalent (Gardasil/Cervavac): coverage and Cervavac facts?
back: HPV 6,11,16,18; protects against genital warts, CIN, cancer cervix. Cervavac: recommended 9–26 yrs; India's 1st HPV vaccine (Serum Institute of India, Pune).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: SAGE dosing recommendation for HPV vaccine?
back: <21 yrs: 1 or 2 doses. ≥21 yrs: 2 doses. HIV positive: 3 doses.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: WHO cervical screening protocol: setting, start/end age, and the 'see & treat' logic?
back: Resource-limited settings; screening 30→50 yrs. See & treat: HPV DNA +ve → treat without biopsy; VIA +ve → treat without biopsy; HPV DNA +ve then VIA +ve → treat.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: WHO recommended screening method order, and repeat intervals for negative tests?
back: Method: HPV DNA + VIA > HPV DNA > VIA. HPV DNA -ve: repeat in 5–10 yrs (HIV+ 3 yrs). VIA -ve: repeat in 3 yrs. HPV+ but VIA-: repeat HPV DNA in 1–2 yrs. TOC: LEEP/LLETZ.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ACOG cervical screening protocol (start age, method to 30, co-test after 30, end age)?
back: Resourceful settings; begins 21 yrs. Pap smear every 3 yrs till 30. From 30: HPV DNA + Pap (co-test) every 5 yrs till 65. Abnormal Pap at 55–65 → Pap till 75.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ACOG: when to stop screening after hysterectomy vs after HPV vaccine?
back: After hysterectomy: stop screening. After HPV vaccine: do NOT stop screening.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: WHO 90/70/90 goals for cervical cancer by 2030?
back: 90% of girls vaccinated; 70% of reproductive-age women (30–50) screened; 90% of women with CIN/cancer cervix treated.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pap smear: pre-test instructions and its diagnostic status?
back: Before Pap: no PV exam, no intercourse, no tampon, no air-drying of smear. It is a SCREENING test, NOT diagnostic; abnormal Pap → per speculum examination.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pap smear sampling: Ayer's spatula vs endocervical brush, fixative, contraindications?
back: Ayer's spatula: from TZ near external os (SCC). Endocervical brush: endocervix (adenocarcinoma). Fixative: 95% ethyl alcohol ± 5% ether. Absolute C/I: none; relative C/I: bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Liquid-based cytology: instrument and advantages?
back: Instrument: cervical brush. Advantages: can be used during menstruation, better pick-up rate, same sample usable for HPV DNA testing.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cervical screening tests: most specific, most sensitive, most cost-effective?
back: Most specific: Pap smear. Most sensitive: HPV DNA. Most cost-effective: VIA.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HPV DNA testing: technique, start age, what it detects, and subtyping tests?
back: Hybrid capture technique; begins 30 (earliest 25); detects high-risk HPV but cannot tell subtype. Subtyping: Onclarity test, Cobas test, partial genotyping.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which infections can a Pap smear pick up vs NOT detect?
back: Pap can pick up infections but does NOT detect chlamydia or gonorrhea.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Colposcopy: role, magnification, what it can/cannot see, and acetowhite biopsy?
back: Diagnostic test; 30× magnification; visualises exocervix & TZ but NOT endocervix. Apply 3–5% acetic acid → biopsy the acetowhite area.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Colposcopy green filter — what does it show, and which patterns are abnormal?
back: Green filter visualises blood vessels; mosaic, punctate, reticular patterns are abnormal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Biopsy choice when growth is visible vs not visible on colposcopy?
back: Growth visible: punch biopsy. Growth not visible: colposcopic-directed biopsy (colpobiopsy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: VIA: agent and colour of normal vs dysplastic epithelium?
back: 3–5% acetic acid; normal epithelium pink, dysplastic epithelium turns white.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cancer cervix: commonest & 2nd variety, commonest site, adenocarcinoma site, ages?
back: M/c SCC; 2nd adenocarcinoma. M/c site TZ > squamocolumnar junction; adenocarcinoma at endocervix. Ages 35–39 and 60–65 yrs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cancer cervix: commonest & most specific symptom, commonest cause of death, route of spread?
back: M/c symptom AUB-M; most specific post-coital bleed. M/c cause of death: renal failure. Spread: direct > lymphatic. (M/c cancer in pregnancy = Ca cervix; m/c cancer in females = breast.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cancer cervix IOC: for lymph nodes / parametrium / renal-ureter?
back: Lymph nodes: PET-CT. Parametrium: MRI. Renal/ureter involvement: CT.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Post-coital bleeding workup after per speculum exam (normal vs abnormal cervix)?
back: Normal cervix → Pap smear. Barrel-shaped/indurated/abnormal → colposcopy + biopsy. Bleeding persists → endometrial biopsy (r/o endometrial Ca). Growth seen → punch biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cancer cervix staging: parametrium / pelvic side wall / ureter-kidney / bladder?
back: Parametrium: IIB. Pelvic side wall: IIIB. Ureter/kidney (or CT hydroureter, cystoscopy bullous edema): IIIB. Bladder: IVA.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cancer cervix staging: pelvic LN / para-aortic LN / superficial inguinal LN?
back: Pelvic LN: IIIC1. Para-aortic LN: IIIC2. Superficial inguinal LN: IVB.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cancer cervix: which stages get surgery vs chemoradiation?
back: Early (IA1, IA2, IB1, IB2): surgery. Locally advanced (IB3, II, III, IV): chemoradiation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cancer cervix surgery by stage & age (IA1 / IA2 / IB1 / IB2)?
back: IA1: young conization, old type 1 hysterectomy (no LND). IA2: young radical trachelectomy, old type 1 + LND. IB1: type 2 hysterectomy + LND. IB2: type 3 hysterectomy + LND. LND = pelvic + para-aortic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Radical trachelectomy: what is removed, and delivery after conception?
back: Remove cervix + 80% parametrium, stitch uterus to vagina (fertility-sparing). After conception → delivery by C-section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Locally advanced cancer cervix: main treatment and radiosensitizer?
back: Main treatment: radiotherapy + a radiosensitizer (chemo drug) to increase cell sensitivity. Cisplatin (± pembrolizumab), given once weekly.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cervix radiotherapy: brachytherapy types and external beam details?
back: Brachytherapy (source inside): intracavitary (tumor + paracervical nodes; tandem=uterus, ovoid=vagina) and interstitial (vaginal mets). External beam (source outside): tumor + pelvic ± para-aortic LN, given as IMRT, total dose 45–50 Gy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HSIL with normal colposcopy: management by age?
back: <25 yrs: repeat Pap in 1 yr. ≥25 yrs: cone biopsy (suspect endocervical Ca not seen by colposcopy, in OT under GA) + colposcopic-directed biopsy + endocervical curettage.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of ASCUS vs LSIL vs AGCUS Pap reports?
back: ASCUS: repeat Pap in 1 yr or reflex HPV DNA testing. LSIL: repeat Pap in 6 m–1 yr or colposcopic biopsy + endocervical curettage. AGCUS: colposcopy + endocervical curettage + endometrial biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What do ASCUS and AGCUS stand for?
back: ASCUS: atypical squamous cells of unknown/undetermined significance. AGCUS: atypical glandular cells of unknown significance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Piver-Rutledge type 1 hysterectomy: names, what's removed, and a key risk?
back: Type 1 = simple = total abdominal hysterectomy; m/c type; removes uterus + cervix; m/c hysterectomy to cause ureteric injury.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Piver-Rutledge type 2 vs type 3 hysterectomy?
back: Type 2 = Wertheim's/modified radical — MAXIMUM risk of ureteric injury, done in malignancies. Type 3 = radical hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Adnexal mass on P/V: features of a benign vs malignant mass?
back: Benign: painful, U/L, cystic, tender, reproductive age. Malignant: painless, B/L, solid/firm, non-tender, extremes of age.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: USG signs of malignancy in an adnexal mass?
back: Solid/mixed-texture mass, papillae, thick septa, ascites, enlarged LN, matted bowel loops (peritoneal caking), ↑vascularity on doppler, size ≥10 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Characteristic USG appearances: hemorrhagic cyst / chocolate cyst / hydrosalpinx / dermoid?
back: Hemorrhagic cyst: reticular/fishnet. Chocolate cyst: homogeneous internal echoes. Hydrosalpinx: retort-shaped tube. Dermoid: dot-and-dash / tip-of-iceberg.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: O-RADS scoring 1, 2, 3, 4/5?
back: ORADS 1: follicular/corpus luteal cyst, no malignancy. ORADS 2: risk <1% (endometrioma, dermoid, hemorrhagic cyst, hydrosalpinx — all <10 cm). ORADS 3: risk 1–10% (any of those ≥10 cm). ORADS 4/5: risk 10–50% (USG signs of malignancy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Adnexal mass with NO USG malignancy signs in a reproductive-age woman: role of CA-125 and management by size?
back: IOC TVS. CA-125 not done (raised in benign conditions; >200 IU suggests malignancy). By size: <7 cm → OCP + follow-up USG; ≥7 cm → ↑torsion risk → surgery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Adnexal mass workup in an adolescent vs menopausal female?
back: Adolescent: ↑risk germ cell tumour → AFP, hCG, LDH. Menopausal: epithelial cell tumour → CA-125 ≥35 IU indicates malignancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: WHO classification of ovarian tumours — four categories with the commonest of each?
back: Epithelial (m/c, 90%; m/c serous), germ cell (m/c teratoma), sex cord-stromal (granulosa = estrogen; androblastoma = androgen), metastatic (Krukenberg).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Sex cord-stromal tumours: estrogen vs androgen secreting, and stromal tumours?
back: Estrogen-secreting: granulosa cell tumour. Androgen-secreting: androblastoma/arrhenoblastoma (Sertoli, Leydig, hilus cell). Stromal: fibroma, thecoma (can secrete estrogen), fibrothecoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Proven risk factors for ovarian cancer (by mechanism)?
back: Excess estrogen (early menarche, late menopause, obesity, endometriosis); excess ovulation/incessant-ovulation theory (nulliparity); genetic (Lynch, BRCA1, BRCA2); carcinogen exposure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Controversial risk factors and the role of smoking in ovarian cancer?
back: Controversial: PCOS, HRT, infertility & ovulation-inducing drugs. Smoking: risk for mucinous adenocarcinoma but protective for others.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Protective factors against ovarian cancer?
back: Estrogen-related: physical exercise. Ovulation-related (anovulation): multiparity, OCP (most important), breastfeeding. Surgeries: hysterectomy, tubal ligation, salpingectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Epithelial ovarian tumour subtypes (mnemonic) and the commonest variety's profile?
back: Subtypes: Serous, Mucinous, Clear cell, Brenner, Endometrioid. M/c serous: age 60–70, mostly B/L, non-specific symptoms, poor prognosis, marker CA-125.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Tumour markers and HPE mutations of epithelial ovarian tumours?
back: Markers: epithelial CA-125; mucinous CA 19-9 & CEA. Mutations: low-grade tumour pTEN, high-grade tumour p53.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Commonest benign vs malignant ovarian tumour; commonest in reproductive age & pregnancy?
back: Benign: serous cystadenoma. Malignant: serous cystadenocarcinoma. Reproductive age & pregnancy: dermoid cyst. M/c ovarian cancer in pregnancy: dysgerminoma; m/c cancer in pregnant females: Ca cervix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ovarian tumours associated with endometriosis vs endometrial cancer?
back: With endometriosis: endometrioid variety & clear cell Ca. With endometrial cancer: clear cell Ca & granulosa cell tumour.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pseudomyxoma peritonei: which tumour, what it is, and commonest causative cancer?
back: Seen with the mucinous variety; mucinous material in the peritoneum; m/c cancer causing it is appendix Ca.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Germ cell tumours: rank in frequency, typical age, prognosis, laterality, and subtypes (mnemonic)?
back: 2nd m/c variety; age 10–30, adolescent, good prognosis, usually U/L. Subtypes: Embryonal, Dysgerminoma, Teratoma/choriocarcinoma, Yolk sac.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: GCT superlatives: m/c, m/c malignant, best & worst prognosis, dysgenetic gonad tumours?
back: M/c GCT: teratoma (dermoid). M/c malignant: immature teratoma. Best prognosis: dysgerminoma. Worst/rapid: yolk sac tumour. Dysgenetic gonads: m/c tumour gonadoblastoma, m/c cancer dysgerminoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ovarian tumours and radiosensitivity?
back: Ovary is the m/c radiosensitive pelvic organ, but all ovarian tumours are radioRESISTANT EXCEPT dysgerminoma (only radiosensitive one).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Bilaterality: dermoid vs dysgerminoma vs yolk sac tumour?
back: B/L dermoid ~10–15% (~20%). B/L dysgerminoma ~15–20% (~30%). Yolk sac tumour: 100% U/L.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Tumour markers: dysgerminoma vs yolk sac (m/c, l/c, not seen)?
back: Dysgerminoma: LDH (m/c), hCG (l/c), AFP not seen. Yolk sac: AFP (m/c), LDH (l/c), hCG not seen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Tumour markers: embryonal, choriocarcinoma, granulosa cell tumour?
back: Embryonal: AFP + hCG. Choriocarcinoma: hCG. Granulosa cell tumour: AMH, inhibin B.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Early-stage ovarian cancer management?
back: CT scan (to confirm early stage) → staging laparotomy (TAH + BSO + LN dissection) → adjuvant chemo: paclitaxel + carboplatin ×6 cycles.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Advanced ovarian cancer (stage 3 & 4): resectable vs non-resectable management?
back: Resectable: staging laparotomy (TAH+BSO+LND) → 6 cycles chemo. Non-resectable (spread to liver, stomach, great vessels, intestine, peritoneal caking): 3 cycles neoadjuvant chemo → surgery → 3 more cycles.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ovarian cancer relapse types and DOC for each?
back: Recurrence <6 months: platinum-resistant → paclitaxel + bevacizumab. >6 months: platinum-sensitive → carboplatin + paclitaxel. CA-125↑ during treatment: platinum-refractory → PLD (pegylated liposomal doxorubicin).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Familial ovarian cancer: associations, age, and prevention in BRCA/Lynch carriers?
back: M/c BRCA1 > BRCA2 > Lynch; age 50–60 (10 yrs younger). Prevention: best TAH + BSO after completing family by 40 yrs; 2nd best endometrial biopsy + annual CA-125.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Steps of surgical staging of ovarian & endometrial cancer?
back: Ascitic fluid/NS wash; TAH + BSO; pelvic & para-aortic LN dissection; peritoneal biopsy; infracolic omentectomy; appendicectomy (only in mucinous variety).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Maintenance chemotherapy in ovarian cancer by BRCA status?
back: BRCA -ve: olaparib + bevacizumab. BRCA +ve: only olaparib (PARP inhibitor).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Meigs vs pseudo-Meigs syndrome?
back: Meigs: granulosa cell tumour / Brenner / thecoma / fibroma + right pleural effusion + ascites. Pseudo-Meigs: any other condition + right pleural effusion + ascites.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Characteristic histology: dermoid / clear cell Ca / yolk sac / granulosa cell tumour?
back: Dermoid: tip-of-iceberg, dot-and-dash, Rokitansky protuberance. Clear cell Ca: hobnail nucleus. Yolk sac: Schiller-Duval body (~glomerulus). Granulosa cell: Call-Exner body.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Characteristic histology: Krukenberg / dysgerminoma / hilus cell / Brenner?
back: Krukenberg: signet-ring cell. Dysgerminoma: nests of cells separated by fibrous septa. Hilus cell tumour: Reinke's crystals. Brenner's tumour: Walthard cell nests.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Mature cystic teratoma (dermoid): features, malignancy, m/c component, torsion timing?
back: Teeth/hair present; m/c GCT; malignancy risk 0.2–2.1% (m/c site Rokitansky protuberance, m/c type SCC). M/c component ectodermal. Mx cystectomy (max torsion risk) or oophorectomy if family complete; m/c torsion in 2nd trimester of pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Granulosa cell tumour: effects by age group, and cancer association?
back: Pubertal: precocious puberty. Reproductive: AUB. Menopausal: postmenopausal bleeding. ↑Risk of endometrial Ca → do endometrial biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Krukenberg tumour: commonest primary, route of spread, gross & microscopy?
back: M/c primary: pyloric end of stomach Ca > breast > colon. Spread: retrograde lymphatics. Gross: bilateral, waxy, ovary shape retained, capsulated. Microscopy: signet-ring cells (nucleus pushed aside).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Definition of infertility, and the percentage breakdown of causes?
back: Inability to conceive after 1 year of unprotected intercourse. Female 40–50%; male 20–30%; unexplained 15–30%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When to START infertility evaluation by patient profile?
back: Female <35: after 1 yr. 35–40: after 6 months. <6 months (early) if ≥40 yrs, known uterine/tubal pathology, previous chemo/radiotherapy, or h/o oligo-/amenorrhea.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Basic infertility investigations for all couples (+ extra tests)?
back: All couples: semen analysis; S.progesterone (ovulation) on day 22; HSG (tubal patency). All patients: TSH, prolactin. If female ≥35: AMH (ovarian reserve).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: WHO ovulatory classes I/II/III with examples, and where prolactin fits?
back: Class I: hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (Kallmann). Class II (m/c): normogonadotropic anovulation (PCOS) — most easily treatable. Class III: hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (POI). ↑Prolactin is kept as a separate cause (not in any class).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Female causes of infertility by category?
back: Ovarian (m/c). Tubal: salpingitis, PID, endometriosis. Uterine: submucosal fibroid, Asherman, Müllerian malformation (m/c septate). Cervical: anti-sperm antibody (immunological — now under unexplained infertility).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Post-coital (Huhner/Sims) test: timing and management of a cervical/immunological cause?
back: Done day 12–14. Management: clomiphene citrate + IUI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Tests that CONFIRM ovulation (progesterone, mucus, vaginal cells, biopsy, BBT)?
back: S.progesterone ≥3 ng (m/c used); cervical mucus ferning absent; vaginal intermediate cells; endometrial biopsy (day 26) corkscrew/sawtooth glands; biphasic basal body temperature.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Tests that PREDICT ovulation (follicular monitoring & urinary LH)?
back: Follicular monitoring from day 10 by TVS — signs: ↓follicle size, fluid in pouch of Douglas, trilaminar endometrium. Urinary LH surge → ovulation 24 hrs later (blood LH surge → ovulation 32–36 hrs).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Endometrial biopsy for infertility: pre-procedure advice, indication, sample handling?
back: Use barrier contraception, avoid OCPs. Indication: genital TB. 2 samples — HPE in formalin, AFB in saline. Done day 26.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: DOC for anovulation: overall / PCOS / pituitary ablation / Kallmann?
back: Overall: clomiphene citrate. PCOS: letrozole. Pituitary ablation: hMG. Kallmann: pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Decreased ovarian reserve: tests, the day done, the best test, and management?
back: ↑FSH (≥25 = POI), ↓inhibin B (<45 pg), ↓antral follicle count, ↓AMH. All on day 3 EXCEPT AMH (any day, best test). Outdated: clomiphene citrate challenge test. Mx: IVF with donor eggs. (Menopausal FSH ≥40.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: AMH values: normal, suggestive of POI, diagnostic of POI?
back: Normal 1–3 IU; <1 IU suggestive of POI; <0.5 IU diagnostic of POI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Tubal infertility: IOC, gold standard, and overall management?
back: IOC: HSG (day 7–10). Gold standard: laparoscopic chromopertubation (methylene blue/indigo carmine into uterus, watch for free spillage). Overall Mx of tubal infertility: IVF.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: B/L cornual block: commonest cause, pathological cause, and stepwise management?
back: M/c cause: physiological spasm; m/c pathological: genital TB. Next step: hysteroscopic cannulation → laparoscopic chromopertubation (relieves spasm/releases mucus plug); if block persists → IVF.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: B/L distal block management, and the hydrosalpinx caveat before IVF?
back: Next step laparoscopic chromopertubation: mild block → fimbrioplasty; severe → IVF. Hydrosalpinx (indicates severe block): do salpingectomy before IVF, as the fluid is fetotoxic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Clomiphene: MOA, ovulation trigger, dose, ovulation vs pregnancy rate, prerequisite?
back: SERM — blocks estrogen receptors in brain → ↑LH/FSH → folliculogenesis → ovulation (enclomiphene mediates the anti-estrogenic action). Trigger: inj. hCG. Dose 50→max 150 mg. Ovulation rate 80%, pregnancy rate only 10–15% (blocks estrogen receptors in cervix/endometrium → unsuitable for implantation). Needs intact HPO axis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Clomiphene side effects (incl. the one needing immediate stop)?
back: M/c hot flashes > ovarian cyst formation; vaginal dryness; twin pregnancy (5–8%); OHSS (1%); visual disturbance → immediate discontinuation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Letrozole vs clomiphene for ovulation induction?
back: Letrozole: peripheral action, NO action on endometrium/cervix; better live-birth & pregnancy rates; no hot flashes. Both need an intact HPO axis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Timing of infertility tests (ovulation / ovarian reserve / AMH / HSG / post-coital)?
back: Ovulation: day 22. Ovarian reserve: day 2–4 (day 3). AMH: any day. HSG: day 7–10. Post-coital: day 12–14.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Semen analysis: abstinence, transport, and 2022 WHO reference values?
back: Abstinence 2–7 days; reach lab within 1 hr; analyse liquefied semen (liquefaction 20–30 min). 2022 values: volume 1.4 mL, concentration 16 million/mL, total 39 million/ejaculate, total motility 42%, progressive 30%, vitality 54%, normal morphology 4% (strict Tygerberg).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Most important semen parameter, and terminology (oligo/azoo/terato/astheno/necro)?
back: Most important: morphology > motility > concentration. Oligospermia <16 M/mL (severe <5 M/mL); azoospermia = no sperm; teratozoospermia = abnormal morphology; asthenospermia = ↓motility; necrozoospermia = ↑nonviable; aspermia = absent semen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Two important causes of azoospermia, and commonest (reversible) cause of male infertility?
back: Azoospermia: cystic fibrosis (B/L absent seminal vesicles & vas deferens) and Klinefelter's (hypoplastic testes). M/c cause of male infertility: testicular; m/c reversible cause: varicocele.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Azoospermia hormonal profiles: pretesticular vs testicular vs obstructive?
back: Pretesticular (hypogonadotropic): ↓FSH/LH/T — Kallmann, ↑prolactin. Testicular (hypergonadotropic): ↑FSH/LH, ↓T — mumps orchitis, Klinefelter. Obstructive: normal FSH/LH/T — varicocele, cystic fibrosis, vasectomy, ED/VD obstruction. Single best test: FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Azoospermia with normal hormones: low-volume/↓fructose vs normal-volume/fructose-present?
back: Low volume + ↓fructose (no seminal vesicle secretions): congenital B/L absent SV (CF/CFTR), ejaculatory duct obstruction (transrectal USG), retrograde ejaculation. Normal volume + fructose present: obstruction in epididymis/vas deferens → scrotal USG → resection & anastomosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Major contributors to semen and their secretions?
back: Seminal vesicle > prostate > bulbourethral. Seminal fluid: adds volume, alkaline, adds fructose. Prostatic: acidic. Bulbourethral: secretes only during intercourse.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Klinefelter syndrome: genotype, Barr body, phenotype, testis?
back: 47 XXY; example of false-positive Barr body. Tall, long legs, gynecomastia, ↓IQ, ↓lifespan; small hypoplastic testes (size by Prader orchidometer). (False-negative Barr body = Turner.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IUI: prerequisites and indications?
back: Prereq: sperm concentration ≥10 M/mL, ≥14% normal morphology; OPD; processed sperm 0.4–0.8 mL into uterine cavity. Indications: cervical factor, unexplained (CC+IUI), ejaculatory problems (hypo-/epispadias), oligospermia 10–16 M/mL, vaginismus. (Not under ART.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IVF: stimulation, trigger, retrieval, transfer, success rate, prerequisite?
back: hMG to hyperovulate; D10 follicular monitoring; follicle 18–20 mm (≥17) and 2 follicles → hCG; egg retrieval at 32–36 hrs; each oocyte incubated with 50,000–1 lakh sperm; transfer day 3 (cleavage) or day 5 (blastocyst), 2 cm below fundus. Success 20–30%; prereq ≥5 M/mL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IVF indications and what it CANNOT be done for?
back: Indications: tubal block, Müllerian agenesis (+surrogacy), ↓ovarian reserve (donor eggs), oligospermia 5–10 M/mL. Cannot do for: severe oligospermia (<5 M/mL), asthenospermia, azoospermia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ICSI: how it differs from IVF, indications, and sperm-extraction methods?
back: Same as IVF except a single sperm is injected into each oocyte. Indications: testicular azoospermia (surgically extracted sperm), asthenospermia, severe oligospermia (<5 M/mL). Extraction: TESE, TESA. (FSH best to differentiate obstructive vs non-obstructive azoospermia.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of male infertility by cause (ejaculation/ED/oligospermia tiers)?
back: Retrograde ejaculation/ejaculatory problem/hypospermia: IUI. Premature ejaculation: SSRI. Erectile dysfunction: sildenafil. Oligospermia 10–15 M/mL: IUI; 5–10 M/mL: IVF; <5 M/mL: ICSI. Non-obstructive azoospermia: surgical sperm extraction + ICSI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Absolute contraindications to OCPs (WHO Class IV)?
back: Breast cancer; severe uncontrolled HTN; undiagnosed vaginal bleeding; smoker ≥35 yrs; h/o thrombosis/DVT/MI/stroke; pregnant or h/o cholestasis of pregnancy; hypertriglyceridemia; acute liver disease; diabetes with vasculopathy; migraine with aura; postpartum <21 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IUCD absolute contraindications (all IUCDs, Mirena, CuT) — and what is NOT a C/I?
back: All: undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, distorted uterine cavity, pregnancy, active PID. Mirena: breast cancer (relative: liver disease). CuT: Wilson disease (relative: molar pregnancy). NOT C/I: previous ectopic/PID, nulliparity, HIV+.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Main MOA of each contraceptive: OCP / POP / progesterone implant-injection / Cu IUCD / Mirena / Centchroman?
back: OCP (E+P): anovulation (↓FSH>LH) + thick mucus + thin endometrium. POP: thick cervical mucus. Prog implant/injection: anovulation + thick mucus. Cu IUCD: prevents fertilization (copper → inflammation) + inhibits implantation. Mirena: prevents implantation. Centchroman: endometrium out of phase → inhibits implantation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: OCPs and cancer risk — increased, decreased, no effect?
back: ↑Risk: hepatic adenomas, breast cancer (perimenopausal), cervical cancer (reversible). ↓Risk: endometrial, ovarian, ovarian cysts, benign breast disease, colorectal. No effect: liver cancer. (HRT: no effect on cervical/ovarian Ca.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Missed pills: management if 1–2 pills missed?
back: Take one pill immediately, continue schedule, no backup contraception needed.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Missed pills: 3+ missed in 1st/2nd week vs 3rd week?
back: Both: take one immediately, EC if unprotected sex in last 72 hrs, backup barrier ×7 days, continue. 3rd week additionally: start the next pack immediately on finishing (skip the pill-free interval).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: DMPA: NFP name, dose/route/interval, window, MOA, advantages, disadvantages?
back: Antara; 150 mg IM every 3 months; window 4 weeks; MOA anovulation. Advantages: ↓seizure frequency, ↓sickling in sickle cell. Disadvantages: delayed return of fertility, ↓BMD, irregular bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: DMPA scheduling: on-time/within-window vs after window?
back: On time or within window (2 wks before–4 wks after): give injection, no UPT, no backup. After window: rule out pregnancy first, then give injection + backup ×7 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Missed CuT thread: stepwise next steps?
back: P/S exam → if not seen, USG → if not seen, X-ray KUB → if still not seen, likely expelled.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Missed CuT thread — management once located on USG?
back: In uterus (correct site or LUS): no intervention. Partially expelled in cervix: remove. Partially embedded in myometrium: no intervention. Embedded in serosa: remove. Intraperitoneal: laparoscopic removal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IUCD with pregnancy: complications and management (continue vs discontinue)?
back: Complications: infection, preterm labour, abortion (not teratogenic). Wants to discontinue: remove IUCD + MTP. Wants to continue: thread visible → remove IUCD + continue pregnancy; thread not visible → IUCD not removed.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: OCPs: low-dose definition, 3rd-gen progestins, and their trade-off?
back: Low dose: <50 mcg estrogen (20–35 mcg). 3rd-gen progestins: gestodene, norgestimate, desogestrel — ↓androgenic side effects but ↑risk of VTE.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: OCP initiation timing and postpartum start times?
back: D1–D5: no backup. After D5: backup ×7 days. Postpartum: not breastfeeding day 21; high thrombosis risk 6 weeks; breastfeeding 6 months.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: OCP drug interactions: which enzyme inducers to avoid, and the exceptions?
back: Avoid with enzyme inducers: anti-epileptics, rifampicin, griseofulvin, anti-retrovirals. Exceptions (can be given): lamotrigine (↓efficacy), levetiracetam, valproate.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Transdermal contraceptive patch: composition, MOA, schedule, backup rule?
back: E+P; MOA anovulation; same C/I as OCP; reversible; no STD protection. New patch weekly ×3 wks then 1 patch-free week. Backup: within 48 hrs of removal not needed; after 48 hrs required ×7 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: CuT vs Mirena: MOA, PID risk, blood loss, emergency-contraceptive use?
back: MOA: CuT prevents fertilization, Mirena prevents implantation. PID risk: CuT ↑ at insertion, Mirena ↓ (progesterone → thick mucus impermeable to sperm). Blood loss: CuT ↑, Mirena ↓. Emergency contraceptive: CuT yes, Mirena no.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Mirena non-contraceptive advantages, and management of PID after CuT insertion?
back: Mirena: ↓endometrial hyperplasia, ↓endometrial Ca risk, ↓blood loss, manages AUB. PID after CuT: antibiotics → review 48–72 hrs → if no improvement, remove IUCD + continue antibiotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: LARC methods, and which common method is NOT a LARC?
back: LARC: implants, IUCDs, injections. Tubal ligation is a permanent method, NOT a LARC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Progesterone-only pill (POP)/minipill: uses, component, MOA, key rules?
back: Used when estrogen C/I; COC of choice in breastfeeding; high failure rate; s/e irregular bleeding; no pill-free period. Minipill component norethindrone (m/c, MOA thick mucus); desogestrel minipill works by anovulation. Norethindrone POP: 1 tablet same time daily, safe period 3 hrs, missed >3 hrs → backup ×2 days, pack of 28.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Emergency contraception drugs (and what is NOT an EC)?
back: Levonorgestrel 1.5 mg single/Plan B (m/c); OCPs 2+2 tablets 12 hrs apart (Yuzpe); Cu IUCD; Centchroman 2+2 (60 mg); Ulipristal 30 mg; Mifepristone 10–50 mg. NOT EC: misoprostol, minipill/POP, Mirena.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Emergency contraception: timing window, most effective methods, MOA, key facts?
back: Best within 72 hrs, up to 120 hrs. Most effective EC: CuT; most effective hormonal: ulipristal. Efficacy max→least: CuT > ulipristal > mifepristone > LNG. MOA: delay ovulation (CuT prevents fertilization). FR 1%; no STD protection; not an abortifacient; most have bleeding within 3 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Emergency contraception special cases (no bleeding / day 5 / who to avoid which)?
back: No bleeding after 3 weeks → rule out pregnancy (UPT). Presents day 5 after unprotected sex → best CuT, 2nd best ulipristal. Avoid: ulipristal in breastfeeding; hormonal EC with enzyme inducers (use CuT); LNG in obese (use ulipristal/IUCD).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Spermatogenesis: when it begins, site, duration, and where sperm gain maturity vs motility?
back: Begins at puberty; in seminiferous tubules; takes 72 days (70–74). Maturity: proximal epididymis. Motility: distal epididymis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Spermiogenesis: definition and the spermatid→sperm structural conversions?
back: Transformation/maturation of spermatids to sperm (no mitosis/meiosis), 10–14 days. Golgi → acrosome cap; nucleus → head; mitochondrion → middle piece; centriole → axial filament/tail.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Capacitation: definition, site, where it begins/peaks, time, and changes?
back: Process giving sperm the ability to fertilise ova; in female reproductive tract; begins in cervix, maximum in fallopian tube; ~7 hrs (6–8); no genetic changes; alters membrane permeability, influx of Ca²⁺ & HCO₃⁻, renders sperm hypermotile.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Sperm fertilizable lifespan & size; what sperm lack; Sertoli cell secretions in IUL?
back: Lifespan 48–72 hrs; size 50–60 microns; sperm lack endoplasmic reticulum. Sertoli cells (temperature-sensitive) secrete AMH, inhibin B, testosterone-binding protein in intrauterine life.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Oogenesis: when it begins; arrest stages of meiosis I and II?
back: Begins in intrauterine life. Meiosis I arrested in diplotene of prophase; meiosis II arrested in metaphase.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When are meiosis I and II completed, and the hormone for meiosis I completion?
back: Meiosis I completed at puberty, ~3–4 hrs before ovulation (hormone: LH). Meiosis II completed at the time of fertilization.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Definitions: follicle vs ovum vs Graafian follicle (oocyte type in each)?
back: Follicle = primary oocyte + follicular cells. Ovum = secondary oocyte arrested in meiosis II. Graafian follicle contains a secondary oocyte.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Sizes: mature ovum, mature follicle; ova fertilizable lifespan?
back: Mature ovum 120–130 microns (largest cell in the body). Mature follicle 18–20 mm. Ova fertilizable lifespan 12–24 hrs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Follicle numbers: maximum (when), at birth, at puberty; ovulated in lifetime; atresia rate?
back: Max 6–7 million at 5th month IUL (20 wks); 1–2 million at birth; 4–5 lakh at puberty. ~400 ovulated in a lifetime; atresia ~1000/month (apoptosis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Events prior to fertilization, and the zona pellucida sperm receptor?
back: Capacitation → binding to zona pellucida → acrosomal reaction → cortical reaction (needs Ca²⁺) → zona reaction. Receptor: zona pellucida glycoprotein ZPGP3; zona prevents polyspermy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where & when does fertilization occur, and how long do sperm take to reach the site?
back: At the ampulla of the fallopian tube, on day 14 of cycle; sperm reach the site in ~30 minutes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cleavage timeline: 2-cell, 16-cell morula, entry to uterus, blastocyst, implantation?
back: 2-cell at 20–30 hrs → 4 → 8-cell (in zona) → 16-cell morula (day 3) → day 4 enters uterine cavity → day 5 blastocyst + zona hatching → day 6 implantation as blastocyst (= day 20 of cycle). Transport: peristalsis > cilia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Parts of the blastocyst?
back: Inner cell mass (embryoblast) and trophoblast.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pre-implantation genetic diagnosis (PIGD): best material, indications, and exclusions?
back: Best material: trophoectoderm (trophoblast); done on a day-3 embryo. Indications: sickle cell anemia, thalassemia, cystic fibrosis, aneuploidy. NOT for sex determination. Embryo kept in Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺-free medium to soften zona pellucida.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Implantation: site, window, endometrial thickness, and when it ends?
back: Site: upper posterior wall of uterus. Window day 20–24 of cycle (6–10 days post-fertilization). Endometrium 10–12 mm (pinopods, highest IL-6). Ends day 10–11 post-fertilization (day 24–25).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Three factors/molecules required for implantation?
back: Apposition: selectin. Adhesion: integrin (most important). Invasion: matrix metalloproteinases.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Trophoblast (8 days post-fertilization): cytotrophoblast vs syncytiotrophoblast roles?
back: Cytotrophoblast: forms chorion frondosum (fetal side) & chorion; extravillous cytotrophoblast prevents PIH. Syncytiotrophoblast: hormone factory of placenta (hCG, hPL).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Decidua: what it is, hormone favouring it, maternal side, and capsularis-parietalis fusion?
back: Endometrium after implantation; progesterone favours decidualization; decidua basalis = maternal side of placenta; decidua capsularis & parietalis fuse at 14–16 weeks (single uterine cavity).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Arias-Stella reaction: what, due to what, and other settings?
back: Hypersecretory endometrial changes on HPE during implantation, due to progesterone; also seen in ectopic pregnancy and OCP use. (Amnion is formed by ectoderm.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Presumptive signs of pregnancy (felt by the patient)?
back: Amenorrhea, nausea/vomiting, fatigue, increased urinary frequency (T1 & T3), breast changes, quickening.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Probable signs: Goodell's, Hegar's, Chadwick's, Osiander's, Palmer's, Piskacek's?
back: Goodell's: softening of cervix (1st sign to become positive, at 6 wks). Hegar's: softening of uterine isthmus (6–10 wks; bimanual fingers touch). Chadwick's/Jacquemier's: blue-violet discoloration of vagina/vulva. Osiander's: pulsations in lateral fornix. Palmer's: rhythmic painless uterine contractions (not felt by patient). Piskacek's: unequal uterine growth.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Positive (definitive) signs of pregnancy?
back: Fetus seen on USG; fetal heart sounds (handheld doppler 10 wks, stethoscope 20 wks); palpation of fetal parts; fetal skeleton on X-ray (8 wks).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Quickening, ballottement timings, and Hartmann's sign?
back: Quickening (1st fetal movement felt): primi 18–20 wks, multi 16–18 wks. Internal ballottement 16–20 wks; external 20–24 wks. Hartmann's/placental sign: bleeding at implantation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Lightening: what happens at 36 weeks vs after 36 weeks?
back: At 36 wks: uterus reaches xiphisternum → respiratory discomfort. After 36 wks: fetal head descends into pelvis, uterine height decreases → breathlessness relieved ('welcome sign'/lightening, late T3).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: USG signs of early pregnancy: intradecidual, double decidual sac, double bleb?
back: Intradecidual sign: 1st USG sign of pregnancy (interstitial implantation). Double decidual sac sign: inner ring = decidua capsularis, outer ring = decidua parietalis. Double bleb sign: yolk sac + amniotic sac.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Total duration of pregnancy and Naegele's formula?
back: 40 weeks / 280 days (9 months + 7 days). Naegele's: EDD = LMP + 9 months + 7 days (Feb: +9 months then +7 days).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Adjusting EDD for regular cycles longer vs shorter than 28 days?
back: Longer (e.g. 32 d): x = cycle length − 28; exact EDD = approximate EDD + x. Shorter (e.g. 25 d): x = 28 − cycle length; exact EDD = approximate EDD − x.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: EDD for IVF cycles (fresh, frozen D3, frozen D5)?
back: Fresh: oocyte-retrieval date + 266 days. Frozen D3 transfer: + 263 days. Frozen D5 transfer: + 261 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is Naegele's formula NOT used, and the best dating method then?
back: Not used if: irregular cycles, conceived on OCP, conceived during lactation, or unsure of LMP. Best method: CRL measurement on USG.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Antenatal visit schedule (ideal) and the WHO/GoI minimums?
back: Ideal 12–15 visits: till 28 wks 1/4 wks, 28–36 wks 1/2 wks, ≥36 wks 1/wk. WHO minimum 8; Govt of India minimum 4.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Caloric requirement in pregnancy (single figure & national trimester-wise)?
back: +350 kcal in all trimesters. National: T1 nil, T2 +300 kcal/day, T3 +450 kcal/day.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Recommended weight gain in pregnancy by BMI?
back: Normal BMI: 11–12.5 kg. Low BMI: 12.5–18 kg. Obese (BMI >30): 7 kg (5–9).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Folic acid doses: prevent NTD, prevent recurrence, sickle cell, antiepileptics?
back: Prevent NTD: 400 mcg/day (1 mo before to 3 mo after conception, all pregnant women). Prevent recurrence (h/o NTD baby): 4 mg/day. Sickle cell: 5 mg throughout. Antiepileptics: 400 mcg before, 4 mg after conception.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: RDA in pregnancy: iodine, calcium (+iron gap), protein (trimester-wise)?
back: Iodine 250 mcg/day. Calcium 1000 mg/day from T2 (gap with iron ≥2 hrs). Protein: non-pregnant 45 g, T1 nil extra, T2 +10 g, T3 +20 g.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vaccines in pregnancy: safe, special-circumstance, and absolutely contraindicated?
back: Safe (killed): hep A, hep B, pneumococcus, meningococcus, rabies. Special circumstances: yellow fever, polio, typhoid. Absolutely C/I (live): mumps, measles, rubella, smallpox, chickenpox, BCG, HPV. (Avoid pregnancy 1 month after a live vaccine.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Td vaccine schedule in pregnancy, and the Tdap recommendation?
back: Td: all pregnant women, 2 doses (1st at first visit, 2nd 4 wks later); if immunized in last 3 yrs with 2 doses → only a Td booster. Tdap (ACOG): ≥1 dose between 27–36 wks to protect newborn from pertussis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: First antenatal visit investigations?
back: ABO/Rh, Hb, HIV (opt-out), VDRL, HBsAg, blood sugar (DIPSI), urine routine (every trimester), TSH, aneuploidy screening. DIPSI: 75 g glucose irrespective of meals, sugar at 2 hrs; at first visit and repeat 24–28 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is GBS screening done, and CBC/Hb repeat schedules?
back: GBS rectovaginal swab at 35–37 wks. Repeat CBC 24–28 wks (ACOG); GoI Hb at least 4 times. TORCH not mandatory in Indian guidelines.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: USG schedule in pregnancy (dating, NT, anomaly, growth)?
back: Dating/viability 6–8 wks. Nuchal translucency 11–13 wks+6 d. Anomaly/target/level-2 scan 18–20 wks (up to 22). Growth scan 32 & 34 wks. If only one USG possible → the level-2 anomaly scan.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Best USG parameter for gestational age (by trimester) and for fetal growth?
back: GA: T1 CRL > MSD; T2 BPD > HC; T3 combination > FL. Best overall GA = CRL (usable till 13 wks+6 d, CRL <84 mm; most accurate at 7–9 wks). Best for growth = abdominal circumference.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: USG GA formulae and key cut-offs (CRL/MSD)?
back: CRL (mm) + 42 = GA in days; smallest CRL measurable 5 mm. MSD (mm) + 30 = GA in days. Cut-offs: MSD to expect fetal pole 25 mm; CRL to expect cardiac activity 7 mm; hCG to see G-sac on TVS 2000 IU.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Earliest TVS appearance: gestational sac, yolk sac, fetal pole, cardiac activity?
back: Gestational sac 4.5–5 wks; yolk sac 5–5.5 wks; embryo/fetal pole 5.5 wks; cardiac activity (M-mode) 6 wks. All seen ~1 wk earlier on TVS than TAS.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Abdominal circumference plane (PUS), and best fetal-weight estimation?
back: AC plane shows Portal sinus, Umbilical vein (hockey-stick sign), Stomach. Clinical weight: Johnson's formula. USG best: HC+AC+FL+BPD (Hadlock/Shepard). AC ≥350 mm indicates macrosomia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: First USG sign of pregnancy, first structure seen, and structure denoting intrauterine pregnancy?
back: 1st sign: intradecidual sign. 1st structure: gestational sac (true = intrauterine, pseudo = ectopic). 1st structure denoting IU pregnancy: yolk sac.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Anencephaly: rank, defect, key associations, recurrence risk, USG signs?
back: M/c NTD (cranial defect); brain herniates with NO scalp/meninges/hair; earliest USG 10 wks, best at 14 wks; polyhydramnios (absent swallowing); female fetus predominance; post-term > preterm. Recurrence: 4% after 1 affected baby, 10% after 2. USG: Mickey-mouse (triangular face), frog-eye, shower-cap signs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Encephalocele vs spina bifida (defect & coverings/signs)?
back: Encephalocele: cranial defect, brain herniates COVERED by meninges/scalp/hair. Spina bifida: caudal defect; USG banana sign (downward cerebellum) + lemon sign (frontal bossing); seen in spina bifida & Arnold-Chiari malformation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Omphalocele vs gastroschisis (covering & aneuploidy link)?
back: Omphalocele: abdominal contents herniate in a sac; associated with aneuploidy (karyotyping indication). Gastroschisis: contents herniate with NO covering (cauliflower-like); NOT associated with aneuploidy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: For congenital anomalies: screening vs diagnostic USG, and IOC for chromosomal anomalies?
back: Gross anomalies: screening level-1 USG, diagnostic level-2 (target) USG. NTD is the 1st congenital anomaly detected on USG. IOC for chromosomal anomalies: karyotyping.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: First-trimester aneuploidy screening (timing, dual test, NT)?
back: 11–13 wks+6 d. Dual test: PAPP-A + hCG (↑ in Down's). USG nuchal translucency: normal <3 mm. ↑NT (≥3 mm) → karyotyping; if NT ≥3 mm but karyotype normal → fetal echo.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Causes of increased nuchal translucency (≥3 mm)?
back: Aneuploidies (Down > Turner > trisomy 18/13), congenital heart disease, TTTS, genetic syndromes (DiGeorge, Noonan).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Second-trimester biochemical screening: triple vs quadruple test?
back: 16–18 wks. Triple/Bart/Kettering test: AFP + hCG + unconjugated E3. Quadruple test: triple + inhibin A (↑ in Down's).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Second-trimester USG soft markers for aneuploidy?
back: Nuchal fold ≥6 mm, absent nasal bone, short femur/humerus, sandal gap, duodenal atresia (double-bubble sign), simian crease, echogenic intracardiac focus, echogenic bowel, choroid plexus cyst (≥2 markers = positive).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Combined vs integrated test definitions?
back: Combined: dual test + nuchal translucency. Integrated: PAPP-A (T1) + NT on USG (T1) + quadruple test (T2).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: NIPT: what it is, sample/source, timing, sensitivity, and how results are acted on?
back: Cell-free fetal DNA; a 2° screening test (expensive); sample maternal blood, source placental cell apoptosis; done anytime ≥10 wks; highest sensitivity (99%, best screening test). -ve: no further testing; +ve: karyotyping.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Chorionic villus sampling: timing, earliest, <10 wks risk, fetal loss?
back: Best 11–13 wks+6 d; earliest 10 wks; <10 wks → oromandibular + limb defects; fetal loss 1%; may show 2 cell lines if placental mosaicism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Amniocentesis vs cordocentesis (timing, fetal-loss risk, indication)?
back: Amniocentesis: 15–20 wks (best 16–18); early (12–14 wks) C/I; fetal loss 0.5%. Cordocentesis: blood from umbilical vein, fetal loss 3%, only indication Rh isoimmunization, done ≥18 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: USG diagnosis of missed abortion (MSD and CRL criteria)?
back: Blighted ovum: MSD ≥25 mm with no fetal pole. Missed abortion: CRL ≥7 mm with no cardiac activity. (Below these cut-offs → repeat USG in 1 week.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: AFP: source, testing window, causes of LOW vs HIGH levels?
back: Synthesized by fetal yolk sac, liver, GIT; tested in maternal serum 15–20 wks (16–18). ↓AFP ('Diabetic GOAT'): diabetic mother, GTD, obese/overestimated POG, abortion, trisomy. ↑AFP: NTD, abdominal wall defect → next step USG.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Radiation in pregnancy: max permissible, definitive damage, highest-risk window, safe Ix?
back: Max permissible 5 rad; ≥10 rad definitive damage; max risk 8–15 wks. A single X-ray after RTA at 6 wks → continue pregnancy. Safe: USG, MRI. Avoid X-ray/CT. Absolutely C/I: radioactive iodine.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Teratogens: lithium, thalidomide, valproate, isotretinoin?
back: Lithium: Ebstein anomaly (box-shaped heart). Thalidomide: phocomelia (proximal limb defect). Valproate: NTD (m/c spina bifida). Isotretinoin: microtia/anotia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Teratogens: ACE inhibitors, indomethacin, chloramphenicol, warfarin?
back: ACE inhibitors: renal anomaly. Indomethacin: premature closure of ductus arteriosus (C/I ≥32 wks). Chloramphenicol: grey baby syndrome. Warfarin: chondrodysplasia, absent nasal bone, stippled epiphyses (only indication: mechanical valve).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fetal alcohol syndrome features?
back: Growth restriction; abnormal facial features (smooth philtrum, thin vermilion border, small epicanthal folds); abnormal brain development/microcephaly; abnormal behavioural development.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Growth periods and the all-or-none vs most-teratogenic windows?
back: Pre-embryonic: fertilization → 2 wks (all-or-none law: abortion or escapes injury). Embryonic: 3–8 wks (most teratogenic). Fetal: ≥9 wks till delivery. (Growth period counts from fertilization; POG from 1st day of LMP; difference 2 wks.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Antibiotics: safe, C/I in T1, C/I throughout (FAT), C/I in T3?
back: Safe: cephalosporins, ampicillin/amoxicillin/amoxiclav, metronidazole, penicillins. C/I in T1: Nitrofurantoin, Fluconazole, Trimethoprim. C/I throughout (FAT): Fluoroquinolone (cartilage), Aminoglycoside (oto/nephrotoxic), Tetracycline (teeth; doxy if needed). C/I in T3: sulfamethoxazole.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Minimum time gaps before pregnancy: live vaccine, tamoxifen, isotretinoin, previous LSCS, VVF repair?
back: Live vaccine 1 month; tamoxifen 3 months (min 2); isotretinoin 1 month (1–3); previous LSCS 1.5 yrs (min 6 months); VVF repair 1 year.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: General rules for viral infections in pregnancy, and serology interpretation?
back: Transmission ↑ as POG ↑; severity ↓ as POG ↑. IgG+ IgM- = past infection (immune, no Rx). IgM+ ± rising IgG = recent infection (fetal risk); confirm with IgG avidity (low avidity = recent). Non-teratogenic: HIV, herpes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rubella in pregnancy: teratogenicity, CRS window, classic triad, rash?
back: Most teratogenic viral infection; highest risk T1; congenital rubella syndrome only if infection ≤16 wks. Triad: cataract, PDA, SNHL. Blueberry-muffin rash. (Microcephaly NOT seen in HIV/herpes/toxoplasmosis.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Toxoplasmosis: source, classic triad, fetal Ix, DOC before vs after fetal infection?
back: Undercooked meat/unwashed fruit. Triad: hydrocephalus + intracranial calcification + chorioretinitis. Fetal Ix: PCR of amniotic fluid. DOC before fetal infection confirmed: spiramycin; after: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Congenital vs neonatal varicella, and management of a non-immune exposed mother?
back: Congenital varicella syndrome (infection 13–20 wks): cicatricial skin scars, limb hypoplasia, microcephaly, IUGR. Neonatal varicella: mother infected 5 d before to 2 d after delivery. Non-immune exposed: no vaccine (C/I), give VZIG within 96 hrs; if disease develops, acyclovir.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: CMV and Zika virus in pregnancy?
back: CMV: m/c congenital infection, m/c cause of SNHL; periventricular calcification, microcephaly, virus-induced SNHL. Zika: Aedes mosquito, infects placental Hofbauer cells, neurotoxic → severe microcephaly, cortical thinning, ventriculomegaly, joint contractures.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Infections causing hydrops, syphilis outcome, Kassowitz law, and mode of delivery (herpes/HIV)?
back: Hydrops: parvovirus, syphilis. Syphilis → stillbirth (not recurrent abortion). Kassowitz law: as pregnancy losses increase, outcome of congenital syphilis improves. Active genital herpes at term → C-section; HIV → vaginal delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Anticoagulation in pregnancy: coagulable state, AOC, DVT, APLA regimens?
back: Pregnancy is hypercoagulable (all factors ↑ except 11 & 13). AOC: LMWH (doesn't cross placenta; warfarin does). DVT: LMWH. APLA: LMWH + aspirin; APLA + preterm labour (UPI/PIH): aspirin. Post-delivery thromboprophylaxis: LMWH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Warfarin in pregnancy: only indication and the trimester-wise plan?
back: Only indication: previous mechanical valve replacement. <12 wks: LMWH or warfarin <5 mg/day. 12–36 wks: warfarin. 36 wks: switch to heparin (warfarin → PPH & fetal ICH). Day of labour: stop all. Bioprosthetic valve: aspirin.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Antiepileptics in pregnancy: safest, most teratogenic, monitoring, and the 3 cases?
back: Safest: levetiracetam, lamotrigine. Most teratogenic: valproic acid. Clearance ↑ in pregnancy → monitor levels every 4 wks. Case 1 (conceived on AED): continue same drug except valproate. Case 2 (polytherapy): shift to monotherapy. Case 3 (first visit 16 wks on valproate): continue valproate (past the teratogenic window).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Thyroid changes in pregnancy and target TSH?
back: Total T3/T4 ↑, TBG ↑ (estrogen), free T3/T4 normal; α-subunit of TSH ≈ α-subunit of hCG. Normal TSH 0.1–2.5; aim TSH <2.5. Fetal thyroid functional at 12 wks; T1 fetus depends on maternal thyroid; maternal T3/T4 cross placenta, maternal TSH does not.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hypo- vs hyperthyroidism in pregnancy: commonest cause and DOC?
back: Hypothyroidism: m/c Hashimoto's; if she conceives, ↑thyroxine dose by 30–50%. Hyperthyroidism: m/c Graves'; DOC PTU (T1); T2/T3 carbimazole/methimazole; thyroid storm PTU. Methimazole/carbimazole C/I in T1 (aplasia cutis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Antimalarials and antifungals/antibacterials for vaginitis in pregnancy?
back: Antimalarials safe: chloroquine, quinine, artesunate, artemether; C/I: primaquine, halofantrine, doxycycline. Candidiasis: topical imidazole (miconazole); fluconazole C/I. Bacterial vaginosis: metronidazole (avoid T1).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prolactin & lactation key points in pregnancy?
back: Prolactin highest in late T3 (not during breastfeeding); falls after delivery. 1st stimulus for milk secretion: ↓estrogen & ↓progesterone. Hyperprolactinemia in pregnancy: DOC bromocriptine, treat if visual disturbance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hematological parameters INCREASED in pregnancy?
back: Blood volume, plasma volume (↑40%), RBC volume (↑20%) → hemodilution, Hb mass, reticulocytes (↑EPO), plasma protein mass, globulin (TBG, SHBG), all clotting factors (hypercoagulable), fibrinogen, WBC (~15,000), neutrophilia, ESR, CRP.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hematological parameters DECREASED and UNCHANGED in pregnancy?
back: Decreased: hematocrit/PCV, Hb conc, plasma protein conc, albumin, A:G ratio (1.7:1 → 1:1), factors 11 & 13, fibrinolytic activity (protein C/S, antithrombin), platelets (benign gestational thrombocytopenia), eosinophils. Unchanged: bleeding time, clotting time, MCHC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Non-blood physiological changes: spleen, pituitary, BMR, osmolarity?
back: Spleen ↑50%; pituitary ↑125–135%; BMR ↑10–20%; plasma osmolarity & oncotic pressure ↓ (hemodilution).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Physiological vs pathological anemia in pregnancy, and the commonest causes?
back: Physiological (Hb ≥11 g%): hemodilution, normocytic normochromic — m/c cause of anemia in pregnancy. Pathological (Hb <11 g%): iron deficiency (m/c), microcytic hypochromic. Anemia is the m/c indirect cause of maternal mortality in India.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: WHO classification of anemia in pregnancy (mild/moderate/severe/very severe)?
back: Mild 10–10.9 g%; moderate 7–9.9 g%; severe <7 g%; very severe <4 g%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IFA pill composition/colour, and folic acid's role by trimester?
back: IFA pill: iron 60 mg + folic acid 500 mcg, red coloured. Folic acid: first 12 wks prevents NTD; from 4th month prevents megaloblastic anemia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IFA dosing to PREVENT vs TREAT anemia (timing & dose)?
back: Prevent: 1 pill/day from 4th month, continue throughout (≥180 days in pregnancy and till 180 days after delivery). Treat: 2 pills/day started when anemia is detected.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Parenteral iron: when C/I, commonest used, best form, and Hb response rate?
back: C/I in T1. M/c used: iron sucrose IV (max 200 mg/day, 600 mg/week). Best: iron carboxymaltose (max 1000 mg/day). Hb rises ~0.7 g/dL per week after 3 weeks (same for oral & parenteral).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ganzoni formula and indications for blood transfusion in pregnancy anemia?
back: Ganzoni: 2.4 × pre-pregnancy weight × Hb deficit (14 − patient Hb) + 500 mg. Transfuse if: unstable vitals, signs of heart failure, thalassemia (NESTROFT+), or Hb <5 g/dL at any GA. 1 PRBC ↑Hb ~1 g% in 24 hrs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of anemia in T2/T3: mild-moderate vs severe?
back: Mild-moderate (7–10.9): <34 wks oral iron (2 IFA/day), >34 wks parenteral iron. Severe (≤6.9): Hb <5 → transfusion; Hb 5–6.9 → ≥34 wks transfusion, <34 wks parenteral iron.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Renal changes in pregnancy?
back: Kidney size ↑1 cm; B/L hydroureter (progesterone smooth-muscle relaxation, worse on right due to dextrorotation); ↑renal blood flow, ↑GFR, ↑filtration; ↓urea/uric acid/creatinine; glycosuria physiological (±).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Respiratory mechanical changes and which volumes increase/stay same?
back: Chest transverse diameter ↑2 cm, diaphragm up 4 cm, chest circumference ↑6 cm, subcostal angle 68°→103°. ↑: inspiratory capacity, tidal volume, minute ventilation. Unchanged: IRV, respiratory rate, vital capacity. Rest decrease.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Oxygen-related respiratory changes in pregnancy?
back: 2,3-DPG ↑; tissue O₂ demand ↑; arteriovenous O₂ gradient ↓; maternal Hb-O₂ dissociation curve shifts to the right.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cardiovascular parameters: increased, decreased, same?
back: ↑: cardiac output, stroke volume, heart rate, femoral venous pressure (varicose veins/hemorrhoids). ↓: peripheral vascular resistance (progesterone), systolic & diastolic BP, MAP. Same: JVP, LV ejection fraction, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Normal cardiac findings in pregnancy that do NOT indicate heart disease?
back: PR ↑, BP ↓, JVP constant, S1 loud + split, S2 normal, S3 easily heard; ECG left axis deviation, mild ST depression, Q in II/III; CXR mild cardiomegaly + straightened left heart border; ejection systolic murmur grade 1–2, continuous (mammary) murmur.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Indicators of true heart disease in pregnancy?
back: ↑JVP; loud split S2 + S4; ejection systolic murmur ≥grade 3/6; diastolic murmur (physiological in 20%); marked cardiomegaly. Symptoms: hemoptysis, progressive dyspnea, non-dependent edema, orthopnea, PND, chest pain.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cardiac output timeline and maximum chance of heart failure?
back: CO ↑ from 5th week, max at 28–32 wks; normalises ~10 days post-delivery. Max heart-failure risk: immediate postpartum > 2nd stage > late 1st stage > 28–32 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Supine hypotension syndrome and C-section positioning?
back: Lightheadedness lying supine (~32 wks), normal on standing; advise left lateral position. For C-section: supine with a pillow under the right hip (↑venous return).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: BP trend in pregnancy and physiological edema features?
back: BP max decrease 24–36 wks, then rises to pre-pregnancy levels in T3 (m/c time to diagnose pre-eclampsia: T3). Physiological edema: fluid retention ~6.5 L, dependent parts, disappears after rest, pitting.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Commonest heart diseases in pregnancy (overall, 2nd, congenital valvular, cyanotic)?
back: M/c: rheumatic heart disease (lesion mitral stenosis). 2nd: congenital (lesion ASD). M/c congenital valvular: mitral valve prolapse. M/c cyanotic congenital: tetralogy of Fallot.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Heart disease with max maternal mortality, and m/c cause of maternal death among HD?
back: Max mortality: Eisenmenger syndrome (2° pulmonary HTN, ~50% mortality, pregnancy C/I; death at delivery or within 1 week). M/c maternal mortality among HD: mitral stenosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Conditions in which pregnancy is contraindicated (cardiac)?
back: LVEF <35%, severe MS (<1 cm²), severe AS (<1 cm²), NYHA 3/4, Marfan with aortic root ≥4 cm, coarctation with aortic valve involvement, pulmonary HTN (primary/Eisenmenger), peripartum cardiomyopathy with residual defects, Fontan with residual defect.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of heart disease in labour (mode, induction, position, analgesia, 3rd stage)?
back: Preferred vaginal delivery; induce (ripe cervix oxytocin, unripe Foley/misoprostol; dinoprostone relative C/I); semi-recumbent with left lateral tilt; epidural analgesia mandatory; IV fluids 1 mL/kg/hr; restrict P/V; cut short 2nd stage (forceps > vacuum); 3rd stage — methylergometrine C/I, use oxytocin (diuretics in mitral stenosis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Mitral stenosis in pregnancy — medical vs surgical management?
back: Medical: limit exercise, prevent tachycardia (β-blockers), diuretics, digoxin (if AF), LMWH (if high VTE risk). On failure: percutaneous mitral balloon valvotomy (best in T2). Valve replacement C/I in pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Peripartum cardiomyopathy: definition, associations, IOC, management, recurrence?
back: Heart failure in last month or <5 months postpartum with no prior heart disease. ↑in twin pregnancy, pre-eclampsia, obesity. IOC echo (LVEF <45%). Mx bromocriptine + anticoagulants if LVEF <35%. High recurrence; future pregnancy C/I if LVEF <50% or residual defect.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Maternal condition → fetal heart disease associations?
back: Pregestational diabetes → VSD. Syphilis + anti-Ro/anti-La → congenital heart block. Lithium → Ebstein anomaly. Congenital rubella → PDA. Turner's → bicuspid aortic valve. Down's → endocardial cushion defect > VSD > ASD.
