subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Dizygotic vs monozygotic twins: incidence, sex, and risk factors?
back: Dizygotic (2 ova + 2 sperm): 2/3 of twins, same/different sex, RF family history, clomiphene (5–10%), hMG (30%), IVF. Monozygotic (1 ovum splits): 1/3 (1 in 250), same sex, RF IVF; same blood group/HLA/karyotype but different fingerprints.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Monozygotic twin chorionicity by timing of division?
back: ≤72 hrs (morula): DCDA. 4–8 days (blastocyst): MCDA (m/c monozygotic). 9–12 days (embryonic disk): MCMA. >12 days: conjoined twins. (Dizygotic are always DCDA = m/c overall.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IOC and best time for chorionicity, prognosis order, and markers of dichorionicity?
back: IOC: TVS at 10–14 wks. Prognosis DC > MC (DCDA best, monochorionic poor). Dichorionicity markers: 4 membrane layers, thickness ≥2 mm, different sexes, 2 placentas, twin-peak/lambda sign (MCDA shows T-sign).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Conjoined twins: chorionicity, commonest & least common variety, delivery?
back: All conjoined are MCMA. M/c parapagus (lower abdomen/pelvis) > thoracopagus; l/c rachipagus (vertebral column) > craniopagus. MCMA & conjoined → deliver 32–34 wks by C-section (cord entanglement risk).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fetal complications of twins: commonest, the only DZ-predominant one, MC-specific, MA-specific?
back: All complications more in monozygotic; m/c IUGR. Only more in dizygotic: cumulative anomaly risk. Monochorionic-specific: TTTS, TAPS, TRAP, selective IUGR. Monoamniotic-specific: cord entanglement, conjoined twins.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: TTTS: where seen, USG diagnosis, and donor vs recipient features?
back: M/c in MCDA (l/c MCMA). USG: MCDA twins + one with oligohydramnios & other with polyhydramnios (deep vascular connection m/c). Donor: oligohydramnios, anemia, less growth, renal failure. Recipient: polyhydramnios, polycythemia, thrombosis, CHF/hydrops.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Quintero staging of TTTS and management by gestational age?
back: Stage 1 bladder of oligo-twin seen; 2 bladder not seen (anuria); 3 MCA doppler changes; 4 hydrops; 5 death. Prognosis depends on recipient twin. Mx: ≤28 wks fetoscopic laser ablation; >28 wks amniocentesis from the polyhydramniotic sac.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: TAPS and TRAP sequence?
back: TAPS: variant of TTTS — one twin anemic (no oligo), other polycythemic (no poly). TRAP: one twin has no heart; donor perfuses deoxygenated blood via umbilical artery → acardiac acephalus or acardius amorphous.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Time & mode of delivery in twins by chorionicity?
back: DCDA: 38 wks. MCDA: 36 wks (34–37). MCMA: 32–34 wks by C-section. Vaginal possible in DCDA & MCDA; mode depends on 1st twin presentation (cephalic → vaginal; breech/transverse → C-section).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Twin delivery: management by twin presentations, and internal podalic version?
back: Both cephalic: vaginal (don't give methylergometrine after 1st). 1st cephalic + 2nd breech: 1st vaginal, 2nd assisted breech. 1st cephalic + 2nd transverse: 1st vaginal, then OT/GA + internal podalic version. IPV (transverse→breech): only for 2nd twin transverse, done in OT under GA, C/I in previous C-section. VBAC allowed: DCDA, MCDA, 1st twin cephalic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hyperemesis gravidarum: definition, causes, complications?
back: Excessive nausea/vomiting → weight loss ≥5% or ketonuria. Causes: ↑hCG (twins, molar), H. pylori. Complications: hypochloremia, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, B1 deficiency (Wernicke's), vitamin K deficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hyperemesis gravidarum: investigation and stepwise management?
back: Ix: USG. 1st step: dietary modification (small frequent meals, avoid spicy/fatty, don't lie down after meals). 1st-line drug: pyridoxine → if fails, pyridoxine + doxylamine. DOC if severe: admission, NPO, IV fluids, ondansetron.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Superfecundation vs superfetation, and Hellin's rule?
back: Superfecundation: 2 ova released at different times in the SAME cycle, fertilized by 2 acts of coitus (rare in humans). Superfetation: 2 ova fertilized in 2 DIFFERENT cycles (not possible in humans; theoretically till 14–16 wks). Hellin's rule: twins 1 in 80, triplets 1 in 80², quadruplets 1 in 80³.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Placenta: description, weight, placenta:fetus weight ratio, and when weights are equal?
back: Discoidal, deciduate, hemochorial. Weight 500 g; placenta:fetus weight 1:6; weights equal at 17 weeks. Diameter 15–22 cm, thickness 2.5–4 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Placentomegaly: definition and causes; causes of a small placenta?
back: Placentomegaly: thickness ≥4 cm — twins, molar pregnancy, Rh incompatibility, diabetes, parvovirus/syphilis. Small placenta: PIH, IUGR, uteroplacental insufficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Placental sides, functional unit, volume distribution, and circulation establishment?
back: Maternal side (1/5) = decidua basalis; fetal side (4/5) = chorion frondosum. Functional unit: cotyledon. Volume 500 mL (villi 350 mL fetal blood, IVS 150 mL maternal; IVS pressure 10 mmHg). Uteroplacental circulation (spiral arteries) established D15; fetoplacental (umbilical vessels) D17–21.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: hCG: source, subunits, function, doubling time, half-life, and level curve?
back: Glycoprotein from syncytiotrophoblast; α-subunit like LH/FSH/TSH, β-subunit specific. Maintains corpus luteum (like LH). Doubling time 48 hrs, T½ 36 hrs; appears 8 days post-fertilization (day 22). Peaks at 10 wks, plateaus at 16 wks, disappears 2 wks after delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Placental hormones from syncytiotrophoblast: hPL, progesterone, estrogen?
back: hPL (= human chorionic somatomammotropin, like GH/prolactin): insulin resistance, marker of placental function, max at 36 wks. Progesterone: corpus luteum till 6–7 wks then placenta (substrate maternal LDL cholesterol); ↓myometrial contractility. Estrogen: E3 most specific, E2 most common; placenta needs fetal DHEA-S precursor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Placental shape anomalies: succenturiata, circumvallate, battledore, velamentous?
back: Succenturiata: big + small lobe with connecting vessels → ↑APH/PPH. Circumvallate (maternal side overlaps fetal as a ring): APH, preterm labour, low birth weight. Battledore: cord at margin. Velamentous: cord attaches to membranes then vessels (loss of Wharton's jelly). Cord = 2 arteries + 1 left umbilical vein.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vasa previa: association, mechanism, types, IOC, management, contraindication?
back: Cord vessels run over the internal os; m/c with velamentous insertion. Membrane rupture → cord ruptures → fetal blood loss → severe fetal distress out of proportion. Type I (m/c, velamentous), II (succenturiata). IOC: USG doppler. Mx emergency C-section; ARM contraindicated.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Singer's (APT) alkali denaturation test, and D/D of fetal distress after ARM?
back: APT test differentiates fetal from maternal blood (HbF resists acid/alkali; reagent KOH/NaOH). Fetal distress post-ARM: cord prolapse (no bleeding) vs vasa previa (bleeding +).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Chronic HTN vs PIH in pregnancy (timing & resolution)?
back: Chronic HTN: BP ↑ before 20 wks, no proteinuria/end-organ damage; doesn't normalise by 12 wks postpartum. PIH: normotensive woman, BP ↑ after 20 wks; normalises within 12 wks postpartum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pre-eclampsia, gestational HTN, eclampsia definitions?
back: Pre-eclampsia: PIH + proteinuria OR end-organ damage. Gestational HTN: PIH without proteinuria/end-organ damage. Eclampsia: generalised tonic-clonic seizure in severe pre-eclampsia. Early PE: before 34 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Proteinuria criteria and signs of end-organ damage in PE?
back: Proteinuria: dipstick ≥+2 (screening), ≥300 mg/24 hr (diagnostic), or protein:creatinine ≥0.3. End-organ damage (any one): platelets <1 lakh, liver enzymes ≥2× normal, creatinine ≥1.1 mg/dL, pulmonary edema, visual symptoms/headache.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HTN diagnostic threshold and mild vs severe PE?
back: BP ≥140/90 on 2 occasions 4 hr apart (if ≥160/110, repeat after 15 min). Mild PE: ≥140/90 but <160/110, no end-organ damage/impending eclampsia. Severe PE: ≥160/110 OR end-organ damage OR impending eclampsia. NOT differentiating: proteinuria, oliguria, IUGR.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pathophysiology of PIH?
back: Incomplete trophoblastic invasion (extravillous cytotrophoblast fails to replace myometrial spiral artery lining) → ↓IVS blood flow → placental ischemia → release of vasoconstrictors → ↑BP, hemoconcentration, endothelial damage, thrombosis, end-organ damage. Maternal NK cells do vascular remodelling.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PIH markers: increased (vasoconstrictors) vs decreased (vasodilators)?
back: ↑: sFlt-1, endoglin, ↑sensitivity to angiotensin II, TNF-α, thromboxane A2. ↓: VEGF, placental growth factor, prostacyclin I2, NO, angiotensinase.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Risk factors for pre-eclampsia?
back: First placental exposure (primigravida, new paternity, long interpregnancy interval); big placenta (twins, diabetes, molar, hydrops); h/o PE; APLA; obesity, age <18 or >40; calcium deficiency; IVF.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Proven prevention of PIH, and ACOG aspirin recommendation?
back: Proven: low-dose aspirin 75 mg OD, calcium (if deficient), weight loss before pregnancy. No role: low-salt diet, bed rest, antioxidants, fish oil. ACOG aspirin 75–150 mg/day for APLA, chronic HTN, multifetal, kidney disease, pregestational diabetes; start ≤16 wks (12–16), stop at 36 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PIH fetal monitoring schedule (mild vs severe), starting when?
back: From 32 wks. Mild: DFMC daily, NST weekly, BPS weekly, growth USG 3-weekly. Severe: NST daily, growth USG 2-weekly.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Definitive management & delivery timing in PIH (mild/severe/immediate)?
back: TOP by induction; best mode vaginal. Mild PE: 37 wks. Severe PE: 34 wks. Immediate TOP in impending eclampsia, eclampsia, HELLP, abruptio placenta, fetal distress, DIC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of severe PE (drugs + steroid caveat)?
back: MgSO4 (DOC, prevents eclampsia), antihypertensives (prevent ICH), corticosteroids (1st dose). Immediate TOP without waiting for 2nd steroid dose in: impending eclampsia, eclampsia, HELLP, abruptio, fetal distress, DIC. Uncomplicated severe PIH: deliver at 34 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Stepwise management of eclampsia?
back: 1) Prevent fall + secure airway. 2) MgSO4. 3) IV antihypertensives. Definitive: immediate TOP (vaginal). (Impending eclampsia: 1st MgSO4, 2nd antihypertensives, TOP after 1st steroid dose.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of mild PE (setting, advice, what has no role, delivery)?
back: Outpatient; BP twice weekly, fetal monitoring from 32 wks, inform of impending-eclampsia signs, antihypertensive ±. NO role: aspirin, bed rest, salt restriction, MgSO4. Definitive: TOP at 37 wks (vaginal).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Antihypertensives: indication, target BP, severe-PE drugs vs chronic-HTN drugs?
back: Indication BP ≥160/110 (NICE ≥150/100). Target systolic 120–130, diastolic 80–90. Severe PE: IV labetalol (max 220 mg), IV hydralazine (max 30 mg), oral nifedipine. Chronic HTN: oral labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Antihypertensive key points: safest, diuretics, labetalol C/I, absolutely C/I?
back: Safest: α-methyldopa. Diuretics C/I in PE (not C/I in chronic HTN). Labetalol C/I in asthma & heart failure. Last resort: sodium nitroprusside (cyanide poisoning). Absolutely C/I: ACE inhibitors, ARBs (losartan), diazoxide.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: MgSO4: role, MOA, therapeutic range, and ampoule strength?
back: DOC to prevent & treat seizures in PIH; MOA centrally acting (blocks NMDA receptors); not an antihypertensive. Therapeutic range 4–7 mEq/L. 1 ampoule = 2 mL = 1 g (50%).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pritchard vs Sibai MgSO4 regimens?
back: Pritchard: loading IM 10 g (5 g each buttock) + IV 4 g; maintenance IM 5 g/4 hr, given 24 hr after last seizure or 24 hr after delivery; no RFT needed for loading. Sibai (IV only, ACOG): loading 4 g, maintenance 2 g.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: MgSO4: pre-maintenance checks, and toxicity levels (10/12/15/20 mEq/L)?
back: Before maintenance check: knee jerk present, RR ≥12/min, urine ≥30 mL/hr. Toxicity: 10 → loss of knee jerk (1st sign), diaphoresis, slurred speech; 12 → respiratory paralysis/arrest; 15 → cardiac conduction defects; 20 → cardiac arrest. Oliguria is NOT a sign. Antidote: calcium gluconate.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Other MgSO4 facts (neuroprotection, abruptio, FHR), and 1st sign/organ in PE?
back: Neuroprotective in all <32 wks; ↓abruptio; tocolytic at toxic levels; normally ↓FHR (persistent ↓FHR in PE = abruptio). 1st sign of PE: high BP. 1st organ affected: kidney (HPE glomeruloendotheliosis). Retinopathy: Keith-Wagner classification.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Eclampsia: cause of seizure, types, m/c MRI finding, anaesthesia choice?
back: Seizure due to cerebral hypoxia + edema. Types: antepartum (m/c & worst), intrapartum, postpartum (within 48 hr). M/c MRI: PRES (posterior cerebral edema). C-section anaesthesia: controlled seizure → neuraxial > epidural; active seizures → general anaesthesia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: HELLP syndrome: Tennessee criteria and management?
back: Complication of severe PE (80% have high BP, abdominal pain in T3). Tennessee: Hemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes (>2× normal) + Low Platelets (<1 lakh). Hemolysis (any 2 of 4): schistocytes/burr cells, ↑bilirubin, ↑LDH/↓haptoglobin, severe anemia without blood loss. Mx like severe PE → immediate TOP after 1st steroid dose.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cholestasis of pregnancy: associations, symptoms, labs, fetal risks, DOC, delivery?
back: M/c in twins/female fetus; recurrence 70%; RF ↑estrogen. Itching of palms & soles, NO abdominal pain; ↑bile acids & salts. Fetal: stillbirth, cardiac arrhythmia, meconium aspiration. DOC ursodeoxycholic acid; fetal monitoring from 32 wks; TOP 37–38 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy: enzyme defect, features, complications, management?
back: Mother heterozygous, fetus homozygous LCHAD deficiency. Abdominal pain T3, altered sensorium, hepatorenal failure, ↑NH3; complications hypoglycemia, DIC, pancreatitis, renal failure. Mx: immediate TOP.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: MCA doppler: parameter, detects what, interpretation, indications?
back: Peak systolic velocity (PSV); detects anemia. PSV ≥1.5 MoM = fetal anemia; <0.8 MoM = polycythemia. Indications: TTTS, Rh incompatibility, vasa previa.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uterine artery doppler at 22–24 wks vs 11–13 wks?
back: 22–24 wks: predict PIH via diastolic notch — disappears = normal; persists beyond 24 wks = ↑PIH risk. 11–13 wks: pulsatility index — ↑PI = ↑risk of early-onset pre-eclampsia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Umbilical artery doppler: S/D ratio and delivery timing by flow pattern?
back: ↑POG → ↑diastolic flow. S/D ratio: ↓ normal; ≥3 mild PIH → TOP 37 wks. Absent diastolic flow → TOP 34 wks (vaginal). Reversed diastolic flow → TOP 31–32 wks (C-section). Reversal in ductus venosus, or REDF + late decelerations → immediate TOP.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IUGR definition, severity, and early vs late onset?
back: EFW/AC <10th percentile for GA. Moderate 3rd–10th, severe <3rd. Early onset <32 wks (symmetrical, fetal causes); late onset ≥32 wks (asymmetrical, maternal causes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ISUOG criteria: early-onset vs late-onset IUGR?
back: Early: EFW/AC <3rd percentile OR 3rd–10th with umbilical artery PI >95th or abnormal uterine artery. Late: any 2 of — EFW <10th, AC <10th, low growth velocity, abnormal doppler (cerebroplacental ratio <5th, MCA PI <5th [brain sparing], umbilical artery PI >95th). Uterine artery may be normal in late onset.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: SGA vs IUGR; symphysiofundal-height clue and workup?
back: SGA: EFW/AC <10th but NORMAL doppler & growth velocity. IUGR: <10th with ABNORMAL doppler/growth velocity. Symphysiofundal height lags ≥4 cm → USG for EFW/AC → if <10th, do doppler (umbilical artery best); <3rd = severe.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Causes of IUGR (fetal/placental/maternal)?
back: Fetal (early/symmetrical): gross & chromosomal anomalies, infections, twins. Placental: pre-eclampsia, circumvallate placenta, velamentous insertion, single umbilical artery. Maternal (late/asymmetrical): poor weight gain, chronic disorders (anemia, heart disease, SLE), smoking.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IUGR follow-up doppler schedule and delivery timing?
back: Monitoring from 32 wks (earliest 28). USG EFW 4-weekly (severe 2-weekly). Doppler: normal weekly, abnormal twice weekly, AEDF/REDF thrice weekly (NST 6–12 hourly, BPP daily). Delivery: normal doppler EFW <3rd → 37 wks, >3rd → 39 wks; uterine artery PI>95 → 37 wks; with oligohydramnios → 34 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Preterm labour: classification, risk factors, and the 2 preventable situations?
back: Early <34 wks, late ≥34 wks. RF: previous PTB, uterine overdistension (twins, polyhydramnios), infection (asymptomatic bacteriuria, BV), short cervix <25 mm. Preventable in: short cervix ≤2.5 cm and previous PTL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PTL prediction: TVS findings and fibronectin?
back: Previous PTL → TVS cervical length at 16–24 wks (IOC TVS). Predictors: cervix <2.5 cm, U-shaped cervix, fetal fibronectin ≥50 ng/mL in cervicovaginal secretions.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prevention of PTL: progesterone vs cervical cerclage criteria, and case-based approach?
back: Progesterone (12→36 wks) if any of: cervix ≤2.5 cm, h/o PTL/T2 abortion, or cerclage. Cerclage if ALL: singleton + h/o PTL/T2 abortion + cervix ≤2.5 cm. Cases: previous PTL + cervix <2.5 cm → cerclage + progesterone; cervix ≥2.5 cm → progesterone; primigravida + cervix <2.5 cm → progesterone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Established PTL definition (ACOG/FOGSI) and suspected vs established by dilatation?
back: Contractions at <37 wks: ACOG 4 in 20 min or 8 in 60 min; FOGSI 1 in 10 min or 6 in 60 min, + cervical changes. Dilatation 2–4 cm = suspected PTL (Rx by cervical length); >4 cm = established (Rx irrespective).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of established PTL by gestational age?
back: POG <34 wks: steroids + tocolytics (48 hr) + MgSO4 (if <32 wks); no role antibiotics/IOL. POG ≥34 wks: corticosteroids only; no role of tocolytics/MgSO4/progesterone/antibiotics/IOL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity: DOC, regimens, C/I, onset?
back: DOC for lung maturity; m/c India dexamethasone, overall betamethasone. Betamethasone: 2 IM injections of 12 mg, 24 hr apart. Dexamethasone: 4 IM injections of 6 mg, 12 hr apart. C/I chorioamnionitis. Action begins 48 hr after 1st dose.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Tocolytics: purpose, duration, ACOG choice by GA, atosiban, contraindications?
back: Buy time for steroid action; 48 hr only; never after 34 wks. ACOG: till 32 wks indomethacin (s/e premature ductus closure, oligohydramnios), 32–34 wks nifedipine (TOC in India). Atosiban (oxytocin antagonist): TOC in heart disease. Absolute C/I: PTL ≥34 wks, IUFD, fetal anomaly, chorioamnionitis, maternal instability; relative C/I dilation ≥3 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PROM vs PPROM definitions and management?
back: PROM: rupture between 37 wks and onset of labour — sterile P/S (PV C/I), IOL, GBS prophylaxis if rupture ≥18 hr or fever ≥4 hr; no steroids/tocolytics. PPROM: rupture <37 wks — >34 wks steroids + antibiotics + monitoring (TOP 37 wks); <34 wks steroids + antibiotics + tocolytics + MgSO4 (<32 wks) + monitoring. Prolonged rupture ≥18 hr.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cord prolapse vs occult prolapse vs funic presentation?
back: Cord prolapse: cord below presenting part & outside introitus (after ROM). Occult: cord alongside presenting part, not visible (diagnosed by abnormal FHR). Funic presentation: cord between presenting part & cervix with intact membranes (PV 'bag of worms', emergency C-section, ARM C/I).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cord prolapse management (CORD) and how to relieve cord pressure?
back: C-O-R-D: Consider diagnosis (fetal distress post-ROM), Organise help, Relieve cord pressure, immediate Delivery (EMCS). Relieve by: lift presenting part manually / knee-chest / steep Trendelenburg / exaggerated Sims / retrograde bladder filling; stop oxytocin, give tocolytic. If cervix fully dilated + station ≥+2 → instrumental delivery; if FHR/pulsations absent → vaginal delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Amniotic fluid volume by gestational age, and sources by trimester?
back: Volume: max 1000 mL at 32–34 wks, 800 mL at term, 200 mL at ≥42 wks. Sources: T1 maternal plasma; 12–20 wks fetal skin; ≥20 wks fetal urine.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Colour of amniotic fluid and what each indicates?
back: Straw/turbid: term. Green (meconium/biliverdin): fetal distress, breech, transverse. Golden (bilirubin): Rh incompatibility. Tobacco/brown: IUD. Saffron/yellow-green: post-term. Dark red: concealed abruptio.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Oligohydramnios vs polyhydramnios: volume/AFI/SVP cut-offs and severe causes?
back: Oligo: <200 mL, AFI <5 cm, SVP <2 cm; severe cause renal anomalies / posterior urethral valve (keyhole sign). Poly: ≥2 L, AFI ≥25 cm, SVP ≥8 cm; severe cause congenital GI anomalies (esophageal/duodenal atresia, obstruction), cleft lip/palate. M/c cause of both: idiopathic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Causes of oligohydramnios?
back: ↓Transudation (small placenta: UPI, PIH, IUGR); ↓urine output (indomethacin, ACE inhibitors); ↓volume (PROM, post-term, leak after amniocentesis); chromosomal triploidy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Causes of polyhydramnios?
back: ↑Transudation (placentomegaly: diabetes, twins, Rh-ve); ↑urine output (twins, diabetes, Bartter syndrome, fetal anemia from thalassemia/parvovirus/Rh); ↑transudation from fetal skin (NTD, abdominal wall defect); trisomies; TTTS; TORCH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Workup of abnormal amniotic fluid and delivery timing?
back: Fundal height < POG (≥3 wk difference) → level-1 USG → mild no further evaluation; moderate-severe → level-2 (gross anomalies + → karyotyping). Poly: rule out diabetes & fetal anemia (MCA PSV). Oligo: rule out PPROM (sterile P/S) & UPI (umbilical artery S/D). TOP: mild-moderate 39 wks, severe 37 wks. (Nitrazine & fern tests distinguish liquor from discharge.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Complications of oligohydramnios and indication for amnioinfusion?
back: Early: pulmonary hypoplasia (m/c), limb reduction defects. Late: CTEV/club foot, Potter facies, cord compression (variable deceleration = m/c CTG finding → meconium aspiration). Amnioinfusion for persistent variable decelerations.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Amniotic band (Streeter's) syndrome?
back: PPROM → severe oligohydramnios → constriction band tightly wraps fetus → distal digit amputation. (Phocomelia = proximal limb amputation, from thalidomide. Indomethacin treats polyhydramnios but C/I ≥32 wks.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pregestational vs gestational diabetes: mechanism and malformation risk?
back: Pregestational (non-A): diabetic conceives, ↑sugar from day 1 → free radicals → congenital malformation; doesn't resolve. Gestational (type A, Priscilla White): insulin resistance from hPL (no hCG role), significant by 24–28 wks (organogenesis complete) → NO congenital malformation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Diagnostic criteria for pregestational diabetes?
back: FBS ≥126 mg/dL, RBS ≥200, 2-hr PP ≥200, or HbA1c ≥6.5%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Congenital malformations in diabetes: commonest system, commonest & most specific anomaly?
back: M/c system CVS > CNS. M/c malformation VSD > NTD. Most specific: caudal regression syndrome/sacral agenesis. CVS: m/c VSD, most specific (irreversible) TGA, m/c finding (reversible) HOCM.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: DIPSI test (India) procedure and interpretation?
back: At 1st visit + repeat 24–28 wks (all pregnant women); fasting not required. 75 g glucose in 300 mL water irrespective of meals (drink in 5 min); 2-hr value <140 normal, ≥140 GDM, ≥200 pregestational. Min gap 4 wks; if first seen after 28 wks, tested once.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Metabolic goals in diabetic pregnancy?
back: FBS 95 mg/dL, 1-hr PP 140, 2-hr PP 120, HbA1c <6% (max 6.5%), average capillary glucose <100 mg/dL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Delivery timing in diabetic pregnancy by type/control?
back: GDM A1 (diet-controlled): ≥39 wks. A2 well-controlled (drugs/insulin): ≥39 wks. A2 not controlled: 37–38+6 wks. Pregestational well-controlled: ≥39 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management: pregestational vs gestational diabetes?
back: Pregestational: MNT (diet + weight) + insulin + low-dose aspirin <16 wks (PIH). Gestational: MNT alone ×2 wks → recheck 2-hr PP: <120 continue MNT, ≥120 add insulin (check 2-hr PP every 3rd day to titrate).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fetal and neonatal complications of diabetic pregnancy (and what's never seen)?
back: Fetal: hyperglycemia, macrosomia (m/c), shoulder dystocia, ↑abortion/IUD/stillbirth (vasculopathy → oligohydramnios + IUGR). Neonatal: hypoglycemia, hypocalcemia/kalemia/magnesemia, RDS (insulin ↓surfactant), NEC, hyperviscosity, hyperbilirubinemia, polycythemia. NEVER: anemia, mental retardation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Macrosomia: definition, diagnosis, and C-section indications?
back: Birth weight ≥4 kg; RF post-term, diabetic mother, male fetus, maternal obesity. Diagnosis AC ≥35 cm on USG. Mode vaginal. C-section if: diabetic & fetus ≥4.5 kg, non-diabetic & fetus ≥5 kg, or macrosomic baby + h/o cesarean (VBAC C/I).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Shoulder dystocia: definition, sign, and HELPERR management?
back: Delay in shoulder delivery ≥1 min after head; obstetric emergency; sign = turtle sign. HELPERR: Help; Episiotomy + Empty bladder; Legs (McRoberts—flex & abduct thighs, straightens sacrum); suprapubic Pressure (fundal C/I); Enter & rotate shoulder (Rubin II/Woods); Remove posterior arm (Jacquemier); Roll over to all-fours (Gaskin). Last step Zavanelli (push head back + C-section).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Shoulder dystocia: m/c fetal complication, growth hormone, and m/c nerve injured in McRoberts?
back: M/c fetal complication: Erb's palsy (porter's tip hand, C5–C6). Fetal growth hormone: IGF (no role of GH). M/c nerve injured during McRoberts: lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: IADPSG criteria for GDM, HbA1c malformation risk, and folic acid dose?
back: IADPSG (any 1 deranged): FBS ≥92, 1-hr PP ≥180, 2-hr PP ≥153. HbA1c risk: <6.5% none, 6.5% 3%, >9% 15% (best reduction = strict glucose control). Folic acid in diabetics: 400 mcg/day.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: OHAs vs insulin in pregnancy, and insulin facts?
back: OHAs C/I (cross placenta → fetal hypoglycemia) except metformin (for non-compliance to insulin) and glyburide. DOC: insulin, SC route; m/c human premix; 1 vial 40 IU; syringe reusable ≤14 times; store 4–8°C. Dose ↑ as pregnancy advances, ↓ in labour (NPO).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fetal lung maturity tests and surfactant timeline; when is OGTT repeated in GDM?
back: Best test phosphatidyl glycerol; L/S ratio ≥2:1 = mature. Surfactant produced by type II pneumocytes at 20 wks (in amniotic fluid by 28 wks). OGTT repeated in all GDM 6 wks after delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pathophysiology of Rh-negative pregnancy?
back: Mother Rh-ve, fetus Rh+ve. Fetal blood enters maternal circulation → Rh antigen stimulates maternal IgG → crosses placenta → fetal hemolysis, anemia, hyperbilirubinemia, hepatosplenomegaly, placentomegaly, PIH, polyhydramnios.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rh-ve female at first visit: stepwise workup?
back: Check husband's ABO/Rh; if husband Rh+ve → 50% chance fetus Rh+ve → high-risk → indirect Coombs test (ICT) for Rh antibodies. ICT -ve = Rh non-isoimmunised; ICT +ve = Rh isoimmunised.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of Rh non-isoimmunised pregnancy (ICT -ve)?
back: Anti-D at 28 wks (antepartum prophylaxis); deliver 39–40 wks (no methylergometrine, early cord clamping). After delivery check fetal Rh + DCT; if Rh+ve & DCT -ve → postpartum prophylaxis anti-D.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Anti-D: antepartum vs postpartum prophylaxis (dose, timing, aim)?
back: Antepartum: 300 mcg (1500 IU) IM at 28 wks if ICT -ve — protects current pregnancy. Postpartum: 300 mcg IM if fetus Rh+ve & DCT -ve, within 72 hrs (up to 28 days) — protects next pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Other indications for anti-D (T1 vs T2/T3 doses), and what is NOT an indication?
back: T1 (50 mcg): abortion, molar, ectopic, CVS, MTP. T2/T3 (300 mcg): amniocentesis, trauma, APH, manual removal of placenta, ECV, fetal death. NOT an indication: cordocentesis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of Rh isoimmunised pregnancy (ICT +ve): titre and critical value?
back: No role of anti-D. Next: antibody titre (critical 1:16). <1:16: repeat titre every 4 wks, monitor from 32 wks, deliver 37–38 wks. ≥1:16: next step MCA doppler PSV.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Isoimmunised pregnancy: MCA doppler PSV interpretation and next step?
back: PSV ≥1.5 MoM (fetal anemia) → cordocentesis (exact Hb/hematocrit). PSV ≤1.5 MoM (no anemia) → repeat MCA in 1–2 wks, monitor from 32 wks, deliver 37–38 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cordocentesis result management (hematocrit-based), and IUT indications/C-I?
back: Hematocrit ≥30%: repeat Hb/hematocrit weekly, MCA 1–2 wks, deliver 37–38 wks. <30%: ≥35 wks deliver; <35 wks intrauterine transfusion (indications Hb <8 g% / hematocrit <30%). IUT C/I: Rh-ve husband (Rh-ve fetus), ICT+/DCT+.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Anti-D neutralising capacity, Rh antigen timing, and Kleihauer-Betke test?
back: 300 mcg neutralises 15 mL fetal RBC / 30 mL fetomaternal hemorrhage. Rh antigen appears on fetal RBCs 38 days post-fertilization; D antigen on chromosome 1, autosomal dominant. Kleihauer-Betke (acid elution): quantitative test to calculate anti-D dose (HbF resists acid/alkali; reagent citric acid phosphate buffer).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Mode of delivery & cord clamping in Rh-ve pregnancy?
back: Vaginal delivery. Cord clamping: ICT +ve (anemia) → delayed 1–3 min; ICT -ve → early (<1 min).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Hydrops fetalis: when it occurs and USG diagnostic criteria?
back: Heart failure when Hb <5 g% & hematocrit <15%. USG (any ≥2): pleural effusion, pericardial effusion, ascites, skin edema (Buddha sign = scalp edema); plus placentomegaly, polyhydramnios.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Types of hydrops fetalis, and infections that do NOT cause it?
back: Immune: Rh > ABO incompatibility. Non-immune: CVS disease (m/c), anemia, chromosomal disorders, parvovirus, syphilis, TORCH. Infections NOT causing hydrops: herpes, hepatitis, HIV.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: USG signs of intrauterine fetal death?
back: Robert's sign: gas in great vessels (1st sign). Spalding sign: overlapping of skull bones. Ball sign: hyperflexion/hyperextension of fetal spine.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Abortion definitions: abortion, anembryonic loss, missed abortion, stillbirth, recurrent?
back: Abortion: loss <20 wks / <500 g. Anembryonic (blighted ovum): MSD ≥25 mm, no yolk sac/fetal pole. Missed: CRL ≥7 mm, no cardiac activity. Fetal loss <10 wks. Stillbirth ≥20 wks. Recurrent: ≥3 losses <20 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Commonest cause of isolated T1/T2 abortion, most important risk factors, viable/lethal trisomy?
back: M/c cause: chromosomal anomalies (aneuploidy > trisomy > monosomy X > trisomy 16). Most important risk factors: ↑maternal age, previous abortions. Most viable trisomy: 21; most lethal: 16. (Infections cause single episodes; HIV causes none.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Recurrent abortion: causes in order, single most important cause, and the syphilis point?
back: M/c endocrine (hypothyroid, uncontrolled diabetes, PCOS); 2nd uterine (T2>T1: Müllerian—m/c septate, cervical incompetence T2-only, submucous fibroid, Asherman); 3rd APLA (single most important cause). M/c T1 recurrent: chromosomal; m/c T2: cervical incompetence. Syphilis → stillbirth, not recurrent abortion (Kassowitz law).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Investigations to do vs NOT do in recurrent abortion?
back: Do: TSH, TVS, APLA antibodies, karyotyping, OGTT. Do NOT: TORCH test, VDRL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cervical insufficiency: IOC, history-based vs USG-based diagnosis, TVS findings?
back: Spontaneous T2 cervical dilatation from shortening. IOC USG. History-based: ≥3 T2 abortions (no USG needed). USG-based: 1–2 T2 abortions OR ≥1 PTB/cervical surgery + cervical length ≤2.5 cm. TVS: length ≤2.5 cm, U-shaped, internal os dilated.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cervical insufficiency: case-based management?
back: 3 T2 abortions → cerclage + progesterone. 2 T2 abortions → ≤2.5 cm cerclage + progesterone, >2.5 cm only progesterone. 1 PTL/T2 abortion + ≤2.5 cm → cerclage + progesterone. No h/o but <2.5 cm → only progesterone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cervical cerclage types (pregnant vs non-pregnant) and transabdominal indication?
back: Pregnant transvaginal: McDonald (m/c, purse-string), Shirodkar (Mersilene tape), Wurm (emergency/rescue, 2 sutures). Transabdominal (laparoscopic): for failed transvaginal cerclage; remove after family complete; deliver by C-section. Non-pregnant: laparoscopic preferred.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: McDonald cerclage: suture, site, timing, contraindications, removal?
back: Nonabsorbable monofilament purse-string on portio vaginalis; 12–24 wks. C/I: ruptured membranes/chorioamnionitis, bleeding, uterine contractions, fetal demise/anomaly, pelvic infection (relative C/I placenta previa). Remove stitch at 37 wks (earlier if preterm labour).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: APLA syndrome: the 3 antibodies and what they cause?
back: Lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-β2 glycoprotein. → thrombosis (venous/arterial/placental) → fetal loss, UPI, preterm labour.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: APLA Modified Sapporo/Sydney diagnostic criteria?
back: 1 clinical + 1 lab. Clinical: ≥1 thrombosis, OR ≥3 losses <10 wks, OR ≥1 loss ≥10 wks, OR ≥1 preterm <34 wks due to severe PE/UPI. Lab: any 1 of 3 antibodies positive on 2 occasions ≥12 wks apart.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: APLA: single most common cause of what, and most characteristic loss?
back: Single most common cause of recurrent abortion. Most characteristic: 2nd-trimester pregnancy loss.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: APLA management in pregnancy by scenario, and DOC in non-pregnant?
back: APS with thrombosis or pregnancy loss: aspirin + LMWH. APS without thrombosis/loss (h/o PTL only): only aspirin. Intrapartum: stop anticoagulation; postpartum resume in 6 hr (vaginal)/12 hr (C-section). M/c LMWH: enoxaparin (start when IU pregnancy confirmed; aspirin when UPT+). DOC non-pregnant: warfarin.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Complete vs incomplete abortion (with POC passed)?
back: H/o POC passage + uterus < POG. Internal os closed → complete abortion (USG empty uterus). Internal os open → incomplete abortion (USG incomplete passage of POC).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Threatened vs missed vs inevitable abortion (no POC passed)?
back: No POC passed → P/V. Internal os closed + cardiac activity on USG → threatened abortion (uterus = POG; Mx conservative: bed rest, progesterone, no exertion/lifting/intercourse). Internal os closed + no cardiac activity → missed abortion (uterus ≤ POG; Mx evacuation). Internal os open → inevitable abortion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: MTP Amendment 2022: upper limits and the medical board?
back: MTP till 24 wks; contraceptive failure till 20 wks; severe fetal anomalies no upper limit (with medical board permission). Medical board = gynaecologist + paediatrician + radiologist. Single doctor's opinion till 20 wks; two doctors for 20–24 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: MTP: provider qualifications and consent requirements?
back: Till 12 wks: RMP who assisted in 25 MTPs (≥5 as primary). 12–20 wks: RMP with 6-month house job in OBG or 1 yr experience or diploma/degree/DNB in OBG. Consent: female's if ≥18; minor/mentally-retarded → guardian. NOT required: marriage certificate, rape FIR. Records kept 5 yrs; no lower age limit.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Medical abortion regimen (GoI/WHO ≤9 weeks) and the kit?
back: USG not mandatory, OPD. D1 mifepristone 200 mg oral; D3 misoprostol 800 mcg oral/buccal/PV; D15 confirm complete. Kit: mifepristone 200 mg + misoprostol 200 mcg each. Methotrexate: WHO-approved for T1, not by GoI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Suction evacuation vs MVA (cannula, pressure, time limits)?
back: Suction evacuation: Karman cannula (number = uterine size or 1 less), 600 mmHg, max 16 wks, best method 10–12 wks. MVA: 2-pinch-valve syringe, 60 mL, 660 mmHg, up to 12 wks (menstrual regulation till 6 wks). Use MVA where no electricity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: MTP anaesthesia, signs of complete evacuation, and uterine perforation management?
back: Anaesthesia: paracervical block. Complete evacuation: ↓bleeding, bubbles in suction tube, grating + gripping sensation on Karman cannula. Perforation with Hegar dilator: wait, watch, monitor. With Karman cannula: stop, do NOT remove cannula, resuscitate + laparotomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Best MTP method by POG, and T2 medical abortion regimen?
back: ≤9 wks: medical abortion. 10–12 wks: suction evacuation. >12 wks: misoprostol (IP). T2 medical (m/c): misoprostol 400 mcg PV 4–5 hourly, max 5 doses; D&E with ovum forceps + blunt curettage (sharp curettage in molar pregnancy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ectopic pregnancy: m/c site, m/c non-tubal site, least common, and heterotopic Mx?
back: M/c fallopian tube (ampulla); m/c non-tubal ovary; least common C-section scar/cervical. Heterotopic (one IU + one ectopic, often after IVF): always surgical. Named criteria: cervical Palman/Rubin, abdominal Studdiform, ovarian Spiegelberg.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ectopic triad, symptoms/signs suggesting rupture, and P/V findings?
back: Triad: amenorrhea (6–10 wks) + lower abdominal pain (most specific) + P/V bleeding. Rupture: orthostatic hypotension, shoulder-tip pain (hemoperitoneum), urge to defecate; signs shock + distension/rebound/guarding/rigidity + UPT+. P/V: adnexal mass (most important), cervical motion tenderness, soft enlarged uterus < POG.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ectopic risk factors (max, m/c, contraceptives)?
back: Maximum: previous ectopic / tubal surgery. M/c: PID/salpingitis. Contraceptives ↓absolute risk but if they fail, ectopic risk ↑ (m/c tubal sterilization > Mirena > IUCD > POP).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ruptured ectopic management?
back: Always surgical (no conservative/medical role). Stable vitals: laparoscopy; unstable: laparotomy. SOC: U/L salpingectomy. Never done: salpingo-oophorectomy, linear salpingostomy (the latter is for unruptured).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Ectopic: IOC, gold standard, confirmed vs suspicious TVS signs, hCG critical value?
back: IOC TVS (confirmed = gestational sac + yolk sac in tube; suspicious = only G-sac in tube, bagel sign, ring-of-fire on doppler, adnexal mass, empty uterus). Gold standard: laparoscopy. β-hCG critical value: 2000 IU (TVS), 6500 IU (TAS).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Unruptured ectopic with suspicious TVS + β-hCG <2000: repeat hCG at 48 hr interpretation?
back: ↑ by ≥66%: viable intrauterine pregnancy. ↑ by <33% (slow rise): ectopic. Decreasing: abortion. (If β-hCG ≥2000 with no G-sac in uterus → medical management.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Methotrexate for ectopic: prerequisites, dose, and follow-up?
back: Prereq: stable vitals, unruptured, β-hCG <5000, sac ≤4 cm, family not complete, absent cardiac activity (relative). MTX 1 mg/kg IM single dose; check β-hCG D4 & D7 — ↓≥15% success; ↓<15% or rising → repeat MTX (up to 3 times) → if fails, surgery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Surgical & expectant management of unruptured ectopic; FT site facts; culdocentesis?
back: Surgery (laparoscopic): family incomplete → linear salpingostomy; complete → salpingectomy. Expectant (rare): hCG <1000 (best <200), falling trend, no cardiac activity. FT: m/c ampulla, l/c interstitium; duration shortest isthmus, longest interstitium (dangerous site). Culdocentesis: blood that clots = vessel, doesn't clot = hemoperitoneum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When to suspect molar pregnancy?
back: Bleeding in T1/early T2, hyperemesis (± high BP/thyroid storm), uterus > POG, FHS not heard, USG snowstorm appearance, β-hCG higher than normal pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Complete vs partial mole comparison?
back: Complete: 46 XX, monospermic, NO fetal tissue, snowstorm USG, PIH/hyperemesis/thyrotoxicosis/theca-lutein cysts present, uterus > POG, maternal genes absent. Partial: 69 XXY, dispermic, some fetal tissue, resembles incomplete abortion, those features absent, uterus ≤ POG, maternal genes present.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Molar pregnancy: IOC, gold standard, and management?
back: IOC TVS (snowstorm); gold standard HPE. Mx: suction evacuation (hysterectomy if ≥40 yrs & family complete) → sharp curettage + HPE. Serial β-hCG: 48 hr then weekly till undetectable then monthly ×6 months. Pregnancy C/I 6 months; contraceptive OCPs; no prophylactic chemo; theca-lutein cysts resolve spontaneously.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Signs of developing GTN, commonest metastasis sites, and lab criteria for GTN?
back: Signs: persistent bleeding/theca-lutein cysts after evacuation, shock, uterine subinvolution, metastasis. M/c metastasis: lungs > vagina. Lab criteria: hCG ↑≥10% ×3 wks, OR plateau ±10% ×4 wks, OR detectable >6 months, OR HPE shows GTN.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: GTN: commonest type after mole vs after full-term delivery; choriocarcinoma occurs after?
back: M/c GTN after molar pregnancy: invasive mole. M/c after full-term delivery: choriocarcinoma. Choriocarcinoma m/c occurs after H. mole. PSTT m/c after full-term pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: FIGO staging of GTN?
back: Stage I: limited to uterus (low risk). Stage II: spread to vagina (just below urethra). Stage III: lung metastasis. Stage IV: liver/brain metastasis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: WHO risk score for GTN: low- vs high-risk features?
back: Low risk (score ≤6): after molar pregnancy, metastasis to lung/vagina, hCG <10⁵, age <40, metastases <4. High risk (≥7): after full-term pregnancy, brain/liver metastasis, hCG ≥10⁵, age ≥40, metastases ≥8.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Management of GTN (low vs high risk) and EMACO regimen?
back: Low risk: single-agent methotrexate (multidose D1,3,5,7 alternating with folinic acid D2,4,6,8). High risk: EMACO/Bagshawe (Etoposide, Methotrexate, Actinomycin, Cyclophosphamide, Oncovin); brain metastasis → radiotherapy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: GTD: risk factor, HPE villi, conversion risk, tumour markers, lung mets appearance?
back: Risk factor: vitamin A deficiency. HPE: villi seen in complete & partial mole, absent in choriocarcinoma. Conversion: partial mole 4% GTN/1% choriocarcinoma; complete mole 20% GTN/2–4% choriocarcinoma. Markers: PSTT hPL; choriocarcinoma/GTN hCG. Lung mets: cannon-ball (m/c) > snowstorm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Antepartum hemorrhage definition; old vs new classification of placenta previa?
back: APH: bleeding ≥28 wks (viability) till delivery of placenta (battledore placenta does NOT cause APH). Old: Type 1 lateral (edge within 2 cm of os), 2 marginal (reaches os), 3 incomplete (partially covers), 4 complete (fully covers). New: low-lying (edge ≤2 cm from os = type 1); placenta previa (edge reaches/covers os = type 2–4).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Placental migration and best USG timing; Stallworthy sign & dangerous variety?
back: T1/T2 previa migrates out of LUS in T3 in 90% — so USG diagnosis done at 32 wks f/b 36 wks. Posterior variety: Stallworthy sign +ve. Type 2 (old) = dangerous variety.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Placenta previa clinical features (history, P/A, exams contraindicated, Ix)?
back: T3 bright-red bleeding: painless, causeless, recurrent, warning hemorrhage present, NO abdominal pain. P/A: uterus = POG, soft/relaxed/non-tender, FHS easily heard, fetal parts easily palpable, malpresentation (transverse > breech). P/V & P/S C/I. Screening TAS; IOC TVS.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Significance of USG in previa, and the placenta accreta link; m/c cause of transverse lie?
back: USG: confirm diagnosis, detect malpresentation, rule out placenta accreta spectrum. Previa + previous LSCS → myometrial villous invasion → prone to placenta accreta spectrum. M/c cause of transverse lie: overall prematurity, at term placenta previa.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Incidental previa on level-2 USG: follow-up and 20-week advice?
back: Repeat USG at 32 & 36 wks; if persistent → elective LSCS at 36–37+6 wks. 20-week advice: avoid intercourse, medium-severe exercise, heavy lifting, standing ≥4 hrs; report bleeding immediately.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Known previa with bleeding: active vs expectant management?
back: Active (immediate C-section after resuscitation) if: hemodynamically unstable, persistent/active bleeding, in labour, fetal distress/category-3 CTG, or significant blood loss + POG ≥34 wks. Expectant if ALL: stable, no active bleeding, no fetal distress, POG <34 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Johnson-McAfee (expectant) regimen for previa?
back: Admit + arrange blood; corticosteroids for lung maturity; tocolytics if contractions (<34 wks); MgSO4 (<32 wks); anti-D 300 mcg to Rh-ve females; followed by elective C-section at 36–37 wks. (Cerclage not done.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Risk factors for placenta previa?
back: ↑Maternal age, ↑parity, big placenta (twins, succenturiate lobe, diabetes, bilobata), endometritis, h/o C-section/myomectomy, h/o IVF, smoking.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: APH diagnostic algorithm: first step and how previa vs abruptio is distinguished?
back: 1st step: maternal resuscitation (2 large-bore IV cannulas no.14 & 16, blood samples, IV fluids, arrange blood, O2 mask, catheterize). Stable + FHS normal + no DIC → TAS: placenta in LUS → TVS → previa; placenta in UUS + retroplacental clot + jello sign → abruptio. Unstable/fetal distress/DIC → C-section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Abruptio placenta: definition, types, and the thromboplastin effect?
back: Premature separation of a normally situated placenta. Types: revealed; concealed (blood behind placenta → couvelaire uterus); mixed (m/c). Separation releases thromboplastin → uterine contraction + DIC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Abruptio presentation and Page classification?
back: h/o trauma/high BP; T3 abdominal pain + bleeding (dark red, not recurrent, no warning hemorrhage). Page: grade 0 retrograde (retroplacental clot found after delivery); 1 pain+bleeding+FHS normal; 2 fetal distress; 3 fetal death/maternal shock ± DIC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Abruptio P/A findings, P/V status, and investigation?
back: P/A: uterus > POG, tense/tender/rigid, FHS not heard easily, fetal parts not palpable. P/V: NOT C/I (do after ruling out previa). Clinical diagnosis; TVS not diagnostic (placenta upper segment, retroplacental clot, jello sign = shimmering placenta on movement).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Abruptio management by scenario?
back: Fetal distress/maternal unstable/DIC → C-section. Fetal death + mother unstable/DIC → C-section. Fetal death + mother stable → vaginal delivery. + DIC → correct DIC then C-section. No emergency → continue till 34 wks; if ≥34 wks + FHS normal + stable + imminent vaginal delivery → vaginal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Significance of ARM in abruptio, and couvelaire uterus?
back: ARM releases prostaglandins (expedites delivery), temporarily ↓bleeding, aids diagnosis (blood-stained liquor). Couvelaire uterus: concealed abruptio → retroplacental blood invades myometrium → bruised uterus; ↑PPH risk; NOT an indication for hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uterine rupture: clues, P/A, FHS, and P/V findings?
back: h/o previous uterine surgery (C-section/classical/myomectomy); abdominal pain + bleeding. P/A: palpable gap over uterus, fetal parts felt superficially. FHS: bradycardia → absent. P/V: loss of fetal station OR migration of station (e.g. +3 → −2).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Definitions: gravida, parity, abortion (and how twins are counted)?
back: Gravida: total times conceived (past + present). Parity: past pregnancies crossing viability (28 wks in India). Abortion: any loss <20 wks. Twins/triplets counted as 1 for gravida/parity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: GTPAL system?
back: G = gravida; T = term deliveries (≥37 wks); P = preterm (20–36+6 wks); A = abortions; L = living children (twins/triplets counted as 2/3). Multipara ≥2 births; grand multipara ≥5 births.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Decreased fetal movement at 34 wks: screening vs diagnostic test, and NST indications?
back: Screening: modified BPS > NST. Diagnostic: biophysical profile. NST indications: ↓fetal movements, all post-term pregnancies, all high-risk pregnancies from 32 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: NST components (FHR, variability, accelerations by GA)?
back: FHR 110–160 bpm; variability 5–25 bpm (fixed = bad prognosis); accelerations: <32 wks ↑>10 bpm for 10 s, ≥32 wks ↑≥15 bpm for ≥15 s, with fetal movement.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: NST interpretation (reactive vs non-reactive) and next step?
back: ≥2 accelerations in 20 min = reactive/negative (normal; very low false-negative; no further testing). <2 → repeat ×20 min (total 40); still <2 = non-reactive/positive. High false-positive rate → next step biophysical/Manning score.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Biophysical profile: components and scoring?
back: USG over 30 min, 5 components ×2 points each: fetal Tone, fetal Breathing, gross body Movement, Amniotic fluid (SVP ≥2 cm), NST (reactive). Breathing 2 if rhythmic >30 s; movement 2 if ≥3 movements in 30 min.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: BPP interpretation & management by score?
back: 10 or 8 (normal AFV): normal → repeat weekly. 6 (normal AFV): equivocal → ≥37 wks deliver, <37 wks repeat in 24 hr. 8 or 6 (decreased AFV): deliver ≥36 wks (chronic asphyxia). 4: deliver ≥32 wks. 2 or 0: immediate delivery (fetal acidemia). Modified BPS = NST + AFI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: CTG basics: graphs, FHR range, variability, prolonged deceleration?
back: Upper graph FHR, lower uterine contractions. FHR 110–160 (<110 bradycardia; persistent bradycardia bad). Variability 5–25 (absent/sinusoidal = bad). Deceleration: ↓FHR 15 bpm ×15 s. Prolonged deceleration: 2–10 min (cause uterine hyperstimulation by oxytocin/misoprostol); ≥10 min = change in baseline.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Early vs late deceleration?
back: Both gradual (U-shaped), occur ≥30 s into contraction. Early: onset/peak coincide with contraction; physiological (head compression). Late: onset & end AFTER contraction; seen in UPI; most ominous type.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Variable deceleration and severe variable?
back: Abrupt (V-shaped); occurs within 30 s of contraction; sudden dip & sudden recovery; indicates cord compression; no fixed relation to contractions. Severe variable: nadir >60 bpm below baseline lasting >60 s.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Category 1 vs Category 3 CTG, and management of category 3?
back: Category 1 (normal): FHR 110–160, variability 5–25, accel ±, early decel ±, late/variable decel absent. Category 3: sinusoidal pattern (fetal anemia — Rh, TTTS, vasa previa) OR absent variability + persistent bradycardia/late/variable decelerations. Mx: in-utero resuscitation (left lateral, O2 mask, stop oxytocin) + emergency C-section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pelvic inlet AP diameters: true, obstetric, and diagonal conjugate?
back: True conjugate (sacral promontory to upper border of pubic symphysis) 11 cm; obstetric conjugate (SP to mid of pubic symphysis) 10.5 cm; diagonal conjugate (SP to lower border) 12 cm. Inlet transverse 13 cm, oblique 12 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Plane of least pelvic dimension (boundaries & diameters)?
back: Narrowest plane: anterior lower border of pubic symphysis, posterior S4–S5 junction, lateral ischial spine. AP 11.5 cm; transverse (interischial/bispinous) 10 cm. (Plane of greatest dimension 12 cm has no obstetric significance.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Anatomical outlet boundaries and diameters?
back: Anterior lower border pubic symphysis, posterior tip of coccyx, lateral ischial tuberosity. AP 13 cm; transverse (bituberous) 11 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Pelvis PYQ facts: clinically-measured inlet AP, critical OC, diagonal→obstetric, key diameter, subpubic angle?
back: Clinically measurable inlet AP = diagonal conjugate. Critical obstetric conjugate 10 cm (below which vaginal delivery not possible). If diagonal conjugate = x, obstetric conjugate = x − 1.5. Most important diameter for labour: inter-ischial. Subpubic angle acute in male, obtuse in female pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Contracted pelvis cut-offs, Naegele/Robert pelvis, and management?
back: Contracted: OC <10 cm (inlet), interischial <8 cm (midpelvis), bituberous <8 cm (outlet). Naegele's pelvis: one ala of sacrum absent. Robert pelvis: both ala absent. Mx of contracted pelvis: always C-section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Caldwell-Moloy pelvis types (commonest, features)?
back: Gynecoid (♀, 50% m/c): transverse-oval inlet, TD>AP, parallel broad walls, obtuse subpubic angle. Android (♂, 20%): heart-shaped, prominent ischial spines, convergent walls, acute subpubic. Anthropoid (ape, 25%): AP-oval, AP>TD, parallel narrow walls. Platypelloid (flat, 5%, least common): flat bowl, TD>>>AP, divergent walls.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Fetal skull AP diameters: which is longest, and what presents it?
back: AP > transverse. Longest AP: mentovertical (14 cm) > submentovertical = occipitofrontal. Mentovertical seen in brow presentation (LSCS only).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Transverse diameters of skull (ascending) and the three 9.5 cm diameters?
back: Bimastoid 7.5 > bitemporal 8 > super-subparietal 8.5 > biparietal 9.5 (m/c engaging transverse diameter). Diameters that are 9.5 cm: biparietal, suboccipitobregmatic, submentobregmatic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vertex presentation engaging diameters by head attitude?
back: Flexed: suboccipitobregmatic 9.5 cm. Deflexed (neutral): occipitofrontal 11.5 cm. Slightly deflexed: suboccipitofrontal 10 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Brow vs face presentation: skull parts, attitude, engaging diameter?
back: Brow (anterior fontanelle to root of nose, partially extended): mentovertical 14 cm → always C-section. Face (root of nose to chin, fully extended): submentobregmatic 9.5 cm or submentovertical 11.5 cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Moulding grading and its significance?
back: Alteration of skull shape passing through birth passage. Grade 1: bones touch but don't overlap (can be normal). Grade 2: overlap but easily separated. Grade 3: fixed overlapping. Grade 3 + slow progress of labour indicates CPD.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Lie definition and types (with transverse-lie facts)?
back: Lie: relationship of long axis of centralized uterus to fetus (correct dextrorotation first). M/c longitudinal (axes coincide). Oblique: at an angle. Transverse: 90° — m/c cause of cord prolapse; Mx C-section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Presenting part in cephalic presentation by head flexion?
back: Vertex (m/c): fully flexed/deflexed/partially flexed head. Brow: partially extended head. Face: complete extension.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Denominators for vertex, breech, brow, face?
back: Vertex: occiput. Breech: sacrum. Brow: frontal eminence/bone. Face: mentum (chin).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Commonest positions: overall, OA, OP, breech, face; least common?
back: M/c position LOT > LOA. M/c occipito-anterior LOA. M/c occipito-posterior ROP. M/c breech left sacro-anterior. M/c face left mento-anterior. Least common LOP.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Lie changes after 37 weeks — causes; P/V fontanelle findings; attitude?
back: Causes of changing lie: idiopathic > placenta previa > polyhydramnios & abnormal pelvis. P/V: fully flexed head → posterior fontanelle felt; slightly deflexed → anterior fontanelle (sinciput) felt. Attitude: normal flexion; abnormal face & brow.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Station: definition, key stations, and pudendal block?
back: Position of fetal head w.r.t. ischial spine. 0 = head engaged; +2 = forceps & vacuum can be applied. Ischial spine = site for pudendal nerve block (pierce sacrospinous ligament).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: CPD: best diagnostic method, radiological Ix, pelvic assessment, and VBAC decision?
back: Best diagnosis: trial of labour. Radiological: MRI > CT > X-ray pelvis. Pelvic assessment in primigravida at 38–39 wks. VBAC allowed if previous C-section was for CPD but present pregnancy has normal pelvis/no CPD; VBAC C/I if previous was for fetal distress but present pregnancy has CPD.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: First stage latent phase: onset, duration, prolonged values, management?
back: Begins at onset of true labour pains; not plotted on partogram; concerned with effacement & dilation. Avg primi 12 hr, multi 8 hr. Prolonged: primi ≥20 hr, multi ≥14 hr. Mx therapeutic rest (NEVER C-section).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Active-phase onset definitions and minimum dilation/descent rates?
back: Active phase begins: old WHO 4 cm, new 5 cm, ACOG 6 cm; concerned with dilation + descent. Min dilation primi 1.2 cm/hr (descent 1), multi 1.5 cm/hr (descent 2). Old WHO 1 cm/hr.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Protracted active phase vs active-phase arrest management?
back: Protracted active phase: rule out OP position (wait & watch) & CPD (C-section); else augment (ARM + oxytocin). Active-phase arrest: no cervical change for 4 hr with adequate / 6 hr without adequate contractions + ruptured membranes → Mx C-section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Second stage: definition, duration, prolonged values, management by station?
back: Full dilation to delivery of baby. Avg primi 50 min, multi 30 min. Prolonged: without epidural primi ≥3 hr/multi ≥2 hr; with epidural primi ≥4 hr/multi ≥3 hr. Mx: station above +2 → C-section; at/below +2 → instrumental delivery (forceps > vacuum).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Obstructed labour: causes, P/A & P/V findings, management, complications?
back: Causes: transverse lie, conjoined twins. Maternal dehydration, exhaustion, tachypnea, tachycardia. P/A: UUS thick/tender/tonically contracted, LUS thinned/stretched, Bandl's ring. P/V: hot dry vagina, caput++/+++, moulding++/+++, ± hematuria. Mx: immediate C-section, do NOT give oxytocin. Complications: uterine rupture, VVF (after 7–10 days).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Bandl's ring vs constriction (Schroeder) ring?
back: Bandl's (retraction) ring: obstructed labour, between UUS & LUS, 2nd stage, felt & seen P/A, a/w dehydration & fetal distress, relieved by C-section. Constriction ring: injudicious oxytocin → localized spasm, anywhere, 1st/2nd stage, felt P/V, mother & fetus normal, relieved by sedation (if reappears → lower vertical C-section).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prerequisites for labour (3 Ps), adequate contractions, and augmentation when head engaged vs not?
back: 3 Ps: Passenger (fetus), Passage (pelvis), Push (contractions). Adequate: 3 contractions/10 min, 40–45 s each, 200–250 Montevideo units. Augmentation: head engaged → ARM then oxytocin; head not engaged → oxytocin then ARM.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: P/V examination intervals; tachysystole definition, cause, CTG, and management?
back: P/V interval: without oxytocin 4 hr, with oxytocin 2 hr. Tachysystole: ≥5 contractions in 10 min → fetal distress; cause excessive oxytocin/misoprostol; CTG prolonged deceleration. Mx in-utero resuscitation (left lateral, stop oxytocin, O2 mask, terbutaline); when FHR recovers restart oxytocin after 30 min at half the dose.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Augmentation methods and contraindications to ARM?
back: ARM by Kocher forceps; oxytocin 6 mU/min increased every 20–40 min by 6 mU. ARM C/I: maternal HIV, genital herpes, intrauterine death, vasa previa/funic presentation. In polyhydramnios do controlled rupture of membranes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Modified WHO partogram: active-phase onset, alert & action lines, plotting zones?
back: Active phase begins at 4 cm (latent & 2nd stage not plotted). Alert line = 1 cm/hr; action line 4 hr to the right. On/left of alert: normal. Between alert & action: be alert/refer. On/right of action: take action. Small box 30 min, large box 1 hr.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Partogram symbols, and the new Labor Care Guide features?
back: Symbols: C clear, M meconium, B blood-stained liquor; X cervical dilation, O descent. Labor Care Guide (new WHO): active phase begins at 5 cm, active phase & 2nd stage plotted, no alert/action lines (dilation may be <1 cm/hr), upper limit per cm defined, new supportive-care section (companion, oral fluid, pain relief, posture).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Third stage: definition, durations, and AMTSL steps?
back: After delivery of baby until delivery of placenta. Duration passive 10–15 min, active 5–10 min, prolonged ≥30 min. AMTSL (best method to prevent PPH): 1) uterotonic within 1 min of delivery (most important), 2) delayed cord clamping (1–3 min), 3) controlled cord traction (modified Brandt-Andrews), 4) intermittent uterine tone assessment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is NOT part of AMTSL, and the early-cord-clamping indications?
back: NOT in AMTSL: early cord clamping, balloon tamponade, bimanual compression, uterine massage. Early cord clamping: absolute indication neonatal resuscitation needed (birth asphyxia); relative known CHD or Rh-ve with ICT negative (non-isoimmunized). Delayed clamping: preterm, COVID+, macrosomic, post-term, HIV+.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uterotonics for AMTSL (WHO first choice and alternatives)?
back: WHO: oxytocin 10 IU IM or IV infusion. If unavailable: methylergometrine 0.2 mg IM (never IV — hypertension); syntometrine (5 U oxytocin + 0.5 mg methylergometrine); carbetocin 100 mcg slow IV; misoprostol 600 mcg (PGE1) oral/SL/PV. Carboprost (PGF-2α) is for PPH management.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Signs of placental separation (surest & 2nd best), separation plane, key factor?
back: Signs: gush of blood, suprapubic bulge, cord lengthening (apparent & permanent), uterine height ↑. Surest sign: placenta felt in vagina; 2nd best: apparent & permanent cord lengthening. Separation occurs along zona spongiosa; most important factor uterine contraction.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Schultz vs Duncan method of placental separation?
back: Schultz (m/c): separates from centre, retroplacental clot forms, less visible bleeding (evident after complete separation), fetal (shiny) surface comes out first. Duncan (2nd): separates from periphery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Retained placenta: definition and management by contraction status?
back: Placenta not separated ≤30 min after delivery of baby. Inadequate contractions: oxytocin + controlled cord traction. Adequate contractions: manual removal of placenta. If no distinct plane on MRP → placenta accreta spectrum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Placenta accreta spectrum: types, etiopathogenesis, risk factors, presentation, Mx, USG?
back: Types: accreta (m/c, villi superficial to myometrium), increta (infiltrate myometrium), percreta (reach serosa). Cause: defective decidua basalis, absent Nitabuch layer. Two main RF: previous uterine surgery (LSCS) + placenta previa in present pregnancy. Presents intractable PPH/placenta won't separate. Mx hysterectomy (if diagnosed antenatally → C-section then hysterectomy). USG: heterogeneous placenta (placental lakes), absent subplacental hypoechoic area.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Transverse lie: presentation, max risk, and management by timing?
back: Presentation shoulder; vaginal delivery not possible; max risk cord prolapse. Antenatal/latent phase → ECV (≥36 wks); active phase → C-section. M/c cause prematurity; at term placenta previa.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Face presentation: m/c cause, denominator, and mento-posterior vs mento-anterior management?
back: M/c cause anencephaly; denominator mentum. Mento-posterior → C-section (vaginal/instrumental not possible). Mento-anterior → vaginal (engaging submentobregmatic 9.5 cm; head born by forceps flexion, vacuum not possible). M/c in platypelloid pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Occipito-posterior position: m/c position, cause, outcome, best management, P/A signs?
back: M/c OP: ROP; m/c cause deflexed head (2nd android pelvis). M/c outcome: head rotates 3/8 circle → DOA → vaginal delivery. Best Mx: wait & watch. P/A: subumbilical flattening, limbs toward umbilicus, back toward flank, FHS best heard in flank.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Deep transverse arrest: m/c pelvis, level, best management?
back: M/c pelvis android (prominent ischial spines); m/c level ischial spine (where internal rotation occurs); best Mx C-section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Types of breech (frank/complete/knee/footling): commonest, cord-prolapse risk?
back: Frank (m/c, hip flexed/knee extended; P/V buttocks+genitalia+anal; least cord prolapse; m/c primigravida). Complete/flexed (hip & knee flexed; P/V heel+buttocks+genitalia+anal; m/c multigravida). Knee (hip extended/knee flexed). Footling (l/c; max cord prolapse). Overall cord prolapse: transverse > footling > knee.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Absolute indications for C-section in breech?
back: Footling presentation, knee presentation, stargazer breech (extended face), previous C-section, breech with macrosomia, preterm breech. (Best Mx of breech overall: C-section; vaginal = assisted breech delivery.)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Breech maneuvers: buttocks, extended legs, shoulders, after-coming head?
back: Buttocks (flexed breech): groin traction. Extended legs (frank breech): Pinard maneuver (pressure on popliteal fossa). Shoulders: Lovset's. After-coming head (born by flexion; m/c death intracranial hemorrhage): dorso-anterior — Burns-Marshall, Mauriceau-Smellie-Veit (malar flexion + shoulder traction), Piper's forceps; dorso-posterior — Prague maneuver.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ECV: procedure facts and prerequisites?
back: Per-abdominal rotation of breech/transverse to cephalic; OPD; no anaesthesia (tocolytics to relax); for singleton breech/transverse. Rh-ve → anti-D 300 mcg; no routine induction after; continuous fetal monitoring. Prereq: POG ≥36 wks (usually 37), adequate liquor + intact membranes, singleton, normal FHR on CTG.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: ECV: absolute vs relative contraindications?
back: Absolute: oligohydramnios, ruptured membranes, active labour, twin/multifetal pregnancy, abnormal FHR on CTG. Relative: PIH, maternal heart disease, IUGR, previous LSCS.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Bishop score: parameters, max, and cut-offs?
back: Parameters: dilatation, position, effacement, station, consistency of cervix (max 13). ≤5 poor → ripen before IOL; ≥6 IOL can be done; ≥9 max success of IOL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Modified & simplified Bishop scores?
back: Modified: effacement replaced by cervical length on TVS (2–4 cm = score 1). Simplified (DES, max 9): Dilatation, Effacement, Station.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Methods of inducing labour (mechanical & medical)?
back: Mechanical (best): Foley catheter (30–50 mL NS; best for previous-cesarean patients), membrane stripping (↑PG). Medical: misoprostol (PGE1) 25 mcg PV 4-hourly (max 6 doses); dinoprostone (PGE2) — cerviprime gel 0.5 mg 6-hourly (max 4) or cervidil (slow-release 10 mg in posterior fornix).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Contraindications to induction of labour, and IOL in previous C-section?
back: C/I: severe CPD, contracted pelvis, transverse lie/brow/mento-posterior face, fetal distress, placenta previa, classical C-section, previous hysterotomy/myomectomy, active genital herpes. IOL in previous C-section: mechanical methods > dinoprostone (cervidil).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Prerequisites and indications for instrumental delivery?
back: Prereq: full dilatation, station ≥+2, ruptured membranes, episiotomy, consent, empty bladder. Indications: fetal distress in 2nd stage, prolonged 2nd stage, maternal exhaustion. Prophylactic (to cut short 2nd stage): heart disease, PIH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vacuum: advantages, where it can't be used, flexion point, pressures?
back: Advantages over forceps: can rotate head, usable when cervix ≥6 cm dilated. Cannot use in: prematurity (ICH), mento-anterior, after-coming head of breech. Applied at flexion point (3 cm anterior to posterior fontanelle). Initial pressure 0.2, max 0.8 kg/cm² ≤600 mmHg. More fetal injury.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Forceps: types by station, advantages, traction force, applied along, complications?
back: Low forceps ≥+2; outlet forceps +3/+4/+5 (head on perineum, scalp visible; only outlet = Wrigley's). Advantages over vacuum: preterm, mento-anterior, head in breech. Traction primi max 20 kg, multi 13 kg; applied along occipitomental diameter. More maternal injury; fetal complications facial nerve palsy, brachial plexus, corneal injury, IVH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Absolute contraindications to instrumental delivery?
back: CPD/contracted pelvis, obstructed labour, transverse lie, brow, mento-posterior face presentation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Types of cesarean section and rupture risk/VBAC implications?
back: Classical (incision on UUS): rupture 4–9%, VBAC C/I. LSCS (transverse on LUS): rupture <2%, VBAC allowed. Low vertical (Kronig's, vertical on LUS): rupture 2–7% (ACOG VBAC+, GoI VBAC-).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: VBAC: when allowed, contraindications, and the C/I mnemonic?
back: Allowed with 1 previous LSCS (and with twins/polyhydramnios/previous CPD). C/I: post-term, CPD, macrosomia, breech. VBAC C/I if h/o: ≥2 LSCS, classical C-section, myomectomy, hysterotomy, or uterine rupture.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Impending scar rupture vs complete scar rupture?
back: Impending (serosa intact): tachycardia, vaginal bleeding, hematuria, suprapubic tenderness++, contractions++, FHS tachy→brady, P/V station felt → immediate C-section (no free fluid). Complete rupture (all layers): shock+tachycardia, profuse bleeding/hematuria, no contractions, fetal parts superficial, uterine gap, FHS not heard, loss of station → resuscitation + exploratory laparotomy (free fluid present).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Cesarean key points: analgesia level, position, time gap to next pregnancy, elective timing?
back: Analgesia T4 (epidural/painless labour T10). Position supine with 15° left tilt / wedge under right hip. Time gap to next pregnancy: ideal 18 months, minimum 6 months. Elective C-section (non-medical) at 39 wks. Cancer cervix = absolute indication.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PPH definitions (standard, ACOG, clinical)?
back: Blood loss ≥500 mL (vaginal) or ≥1000 mL (cesarean) within 12 wks. ACOG: ≥1000 mL irrespective of route OR bleeding + hypovolemia signs. Clinical: hematocrit fall ≥10% or bleeding needing transfusion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PPH: epidemiology, primary vs secondary (timing & m/c cause), and the 4 T's?
back: M/c cause of maternal mortality in India & of shock after delivery. Primary: <24 hr (m/c atonic PPH). Secondary: ≥24 hr–12 wks (m/c retained placental tissue). 4 T's: Tone (m/c), Trauma, Tissue (retained), Thrombin (coagulopathy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Initial PPH management and the traumatic vs atonic branch point?
back: Resuscitate: massage uterus, 2 large-bore IV (16/14), 1000 mL crystalloids, foley for I/O, CBC + grouping, arrange blood; then P/A → P/V. Normal tone + bleeding → traumatic PPH (check laceration/hematoma → repair). Reduced tone → atonic PPH (ensure placenta complete → uterotonics + tranexamic acid + bimanual massage).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uterotonics in PPH: oxytocin and methylergometrine (dose, cautions/C-I)?
back: Oxytocin 20–40 IU IM/IV infusion (never IV bolus → hypotension/cardiac arrest; not with 5% D → water intoxication). Methylergometrine 0.2 mg IM (C/I after 1st twin, heart disease, pre-eclampsia, eclampsia, Rh-ve, and PPH due to ↑BP).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PPH: misoprostol, carboprost, tranexamic acid, and drugs NOT used?
back: Misoprostol: WHO 800 mcg SL/oral, ACOG 600–1000 mcg (not IV; s/e hyperthermia; C/I previous C-section; asthma not C/I). Carboprost (PGF2α/Hemabate) 250 mcg IM q15–90 min, max 8 doses/2 mg (C/I asthma). Tranexamic acid within 3 hr. Not used in PPH: carbetocin (AMTSL), dinoprostone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Stepwise surgical management of atonic PPH?
back: Uterotonic + TXA + bimanual massage → balloon tamponade (Bakri, max 500 mL, remove after 12 hr; or SB/condom catheter) → compression sutures (B-Lynch m/c; Hayman, Cho) → stepwise devascularization (uterine → ovarian → anterior division of internal iliac artery). Stable: ligate ant. division internal iliac; unstable: subtotal hysterectomy. If vitals become unstable anytime → skip algorithm, go directly to uterine artery ligation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uterine inversion: cause, type of shock, P/A & P/V findings?
back: Immediate shock after delivery; cause mismanaged 3rd stage. Shock neurogenic (hemorrhagic = cause of death). P/A: cup-like depression below umbilicus / uterus not felt. P/V: globular mass (fundus) felt in vagina.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Amniotic fluid embolism: presentation, alternate name, diagnosis, P/A & P/V?
back: Breathlessness/respiratory difficulty/hypoxia + shock; initially less bleeding then DIC phase; aka anaphylactoid syndrome of pregnancy; clinical diagnosis (pulmonary artery catheterization may help). P/A & P/V normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Postpartum visual disturbance + headache + hypoglycemia + shock: diagnosis, causes, Mx?
back: Pituitary apoplexy. Due to prolactinoma during pregnancy + PPH + Sheehan's. Mx: steroids (1st line).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uterine inversion management?
back: Emergency; check placenta. Separated → manual repositioning → fails → uterine relaxant + repositioning → fails → Huntington's/Haultain's surgery. Still attached → stop oxytocin + tocolytic + manual repositioning → repositioning under anaesthesia → remove placenta & reattempt → surgery. Once repositioned: stop tocolysis, start oxytocin, deliver placenta, antibiotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Vulval hematoma: features, artery, management?
back: Difficulty passing urine + severe pelvic pain after delivery; artery = pudendal. Mx: check vitals → conservative (analgesics, sitz bath); surgery (drain) if deteriorating vitals, increasing size, or severe unbearable pain.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Median vs mediolateral episiotomy comparison?
back: Median: fewer fibres cut, less bleeding, quick repair/healing, less gaping/dyspareunia, but ↑anal sphincter risk. Mediolateral (60°): more fibres/bleeding, slow healing, more gaping/dyspareunia, anal sphincter not involved. WHO does not recommend routine episiotomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Episiotomy: indications, muscles NOT cut, repair sequence?
back: Indications: after-coming head of breech, instrumental delivery, macrosomia, shoulder dystocia. Muscles not cut: ischiococcygeus/coccygeus, ischiocavernosus, anal sphincter, obturator internus. Repair sequence: mucosa → muscles → skin.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Perineal tear grading?
back: Grade 1: vaginal mucosa. Grade 2: perineal muscles. Grade 3: anal sphincter (3a <50% external, 3b ≥50% external, 3c internal sphincter). Grade 4: anal epithelium/rectal mucosa. Grade 3 & 4 = complete perineal tear (repair under GA).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Recommended measures to prevent perineal tear (and what's not recommended)?
back: Recommended: perineal massage, warm compress, controlled delivery of head, perineal support, Ritgen maneuver (flex then extend head with one hand, support perineum with other). Not recommended: routine episiotomy, fundal pressure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Postnatal visit schedules (ACOG vs GoI) and uterine involution timeline?
back: ACOG: initial 3 wks, final 12 wks. GoI: day 1, day 3, day 7, week 6. Involution: immediately just below umbilicus (~20-wk size); no change for 24 hr; day 2 one finger below umbilicus, ↓1 finger/day; 1 wk midway umbilicus-symphysis; 2 wks pelvic organ; 4 wks complete.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Uterine weight involution, and timelines for ovulation/menstruation return?
back: Uterus: 1000 g → 500 g (1 wk) → 300 g (2 wks) → 80–100 g (4 wks). Ovulation (non/partial breastfeeding): earliest 28 days, generally 7 wks (5–11). Menstruation: 6–8 wks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rule of 3 for contraception, and contraceptive of choice in breastfeeding?
back: Contraception begins: partial/non-breastfeeding 3 weeks; completely breastfeeding 3 months. Contraceptive of choice in breastfeeding: POP > IUCD (estrogen ↓milk secretion).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Postpartum return to normal (blood volume/CO/GFR/hCG), after-pain, and lochia?
back: Blood volume 1 wk, cardiac output 10 days, GFR 2 wks, hCG 2–4 wks. After-pain: multiparous, due to oxytocin, during breastfeeding, ↓by day 3. Lochia: avg 24–36 days; sequence rubra → serosa → alba (components decidua, blood, leukocytes, exudate).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Postpartum blues vs depression vs psychosis?
back: Blues: sudden estrogen/progesterone withdrawal, mild symptoms (insomnia, fatigue, anxiety), no Rx, onset 2–4 days, lasts <2 wks. Depression: 2 wks–12 months, phobia/anxiety/unstable, Mx antidepressants. Psychosis: <0.1%, onset 2–3 days, attention deficit/confusion/altered sensorium, Mx antidepressant + antipsychotic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Puerperal pyrexia and postpartum endometritis (site, RF, presentation, Mx)?
back: Puerperal pyrexia: temperature ≥38°C (100.4°F). Endometritis (commonest cause): m/c site placental site; RF C-section, chorioamnionitis, prolonged rupture of membranes; presents fever, subinvolution, uterine tenderness, ± foul lochia; Mx broad-spectrum antibiotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Septic pelvic thrombophlebitis: when suspected, m/c vein, Ix, management?
back: Clinical diagnosis; suspect when endometritis fever does NOT resolve with broad-spectrum antibiotics. M/c vein: right ovarian vein. Blood culture negative; CT/MRI may show thrombosed vein; P/V cord-like structure. Mx: add heparin to antibiotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Subinvolution: causes, features, IOC, management?
back: Causes: retained placental fragments, infection. C/F excessive bleeding, prolonged lochia, uterus bigger than expected. IOC pelvic USG. Mx uterotonic followed by gentle curettage (risk of Asherman syndrome).
